**Previously updated:** May 5, 2026-d (D3 RESUME COMPLETED + Auto-Analyze defaults deployed + Option A invisibility VERIFIED LIVE AT SCALE session — THREE new INFORMATIONAL entries: (1) **PROCESS-level slip — batch-vs-keyword miscount in mid-session projection.** During D3 resume launch, Claude said "batch 1 of ~26" when the correct count was "batch 1 of ~204." Conflated keywords with batches in the math (8 keywords/batch × ~205 batches ≈ 1632 keywords, NOT 1640 keywords ÷ 8 = 26 batches). Director caught implicitly by sharing pre-flight output showing 1632 in-scope keywords. Operational lesson: when projecting batch counts, do unit conversion explicitly (keywords ÷ batch_size = batches) rather than pattern-matching to a recalled number. (2) **PROCESS-level slip recurrence — re-asking director to verify settings that persist via UserPreference.** Across multiple sessions, Claude has asked director to "verify Thinking settings before each run" or similar. Settings persist per-project via the UserPreference DB table (per ROADMAP item #8 B1 settings persistence shipped 2026-04-24). Re-asking is unnecessary friction — adjacent to Rule 14g (trust director's setup confirmation). Operational lesson: at session start, Claude should read persisted settings (or just confirm correct in prior successful run per STATE block) and not re-ask. The right structural fix is to make pre-flight + activity-log surface canonical settings on every run (captured in ROADMAP pre-flight visibility entry extension) — once shipped, Claude can point director to visible activity log line instead of asking. (3) **OPERATIONAL finding (NEW failure mode) — Anthropic API credit exhaustion mid-run halted D3 resume for ~36 min.** Batch 85 failed three retries at 8:06:52 PM with `HTTP 400: Your credit balance is too low.` Run halted until director topped up at 8:42:28 PM; resumed cleanly. **No proactive warning before failure.** This is a NEW failure mode not previously documented — long unattended runs can be silently halted by API-credit exhaustion. Director was available to top up; another time they might not be. Captured as ROADMAP NEW MEDIUM "Auto-Analyze cost forecasting + credit-balance check" with sliding-window estimator algorithm spec covering pre-flight check + in-run heartbeat + abort threshold. Operational lesson at the platform-discipline level: long unattended runs need a credit-budget gate at launch + in-flight heartbeat warning; estimating run cost vs. credit balance before launching is a structural-defense pattern that generalizes beyond Auto-Analyze to any future long-running AI workflow.)
**Last updated in session:** session_2026-05-05-d_d3-resume-completed-and-defaults-deployed (Claude Code)
**Previously updated:** May 5, 2026-c (Shared Workflow Components Library Phase-1 build session — ONE INFORMATIONAL entry: OPERATIONAL slip — **Next.js App Router private-folder convention surprised initial smoke-test page placement.** When the Phase-1 components library smoke-test page was first written, Claude placed it at `src/app/__components-smoke-test/page.tsx` thinking the leading double-underscore would visually flag the path as "internal use only." The build succeeded but the route did not appear in the route list — Next.js App Router treats folders prefixed with `_` (single OR double underscore) as **private folders** that opt out of routing entirely. Captured in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/01-getting-started/02-project-structure.md` as one of the routing conventions; Claude had not read this file before writing the page. The fix was a one-command rename to `src/app/components-smoke-test/page.tsx` — route registered correctly on the next build. **Operational lesson for future Claude Code sessions touching App Router routing:** when adding a new route, verify the folder name doesn't start with `_` (private folder), `(` (route group — folder name in parens is excluded from URL path), or `@` (parallel route slot). The CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER's mandate to "Read the relevant guide in `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` before writing any code" applies particularly to folder-naming decisions in `src/app/`. NOT a critical mistake — the slip was caught immediately when the build output didn't show the new route, then fixed in under a minute. Cross-references: AGENTS.md ("This is NOT the Next.js you know"), `node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/01-getting-started/02-project-structure.md`. ONE new INFORMATIONAL entry only.)
**Last updated in session:** session_2026-05-05-c_components-library-phase-1-build (Claude Code)
**Previously updated:** May 5, 2026-b (Sister-link consolidation drift cleanup session — ONE new entry: PROCESS-level slip — **defense-in-depth-as-default-pattern over-prescription, caught mid-session by director's "don't think about pink elephants" architectural challenge.** When Claude began the cleanup, the original framing was the standard defense-in-depth pattern this codebase uses elsewhere (the 2026-05-03-c regular-batch sister-link deferral did this; the 2026-05-01 ADD_TOPIC + ADD_KEYWORD lock did this) — explicit prompt forbiddance + applier-side rejection + tests. Claude executed the pattern: prompt edits enumerated `ADD_SISTER_LINK` and `REMOVE_SISTER_LINK` in multiple FORBIDDEN-OPERATIONS sections with explicit deferral-rationale prose; applier `consolidationMode` was tightened to reject the four ops; tests were added. After the Read-It-Back of the deferral framing, director surfaced the architectural concern: *"Why are we mentioning sister-links at all in any prompts? Wouldn't that further confuse the model? I want you to think about whether it is better to simply not mention sister links at all in the prompt so that sister links are not even a part of the thinking process in the Auto Analyze."* The challenge surfaced two separate failure modes: (a) **explicit forbiddance is a "pink elephants" priming pattern** — telling a model "do NOT emit X" still makes the model think about X; combined with the visible Sister Nodes column in TSV input AND prior V4 prompts having included sister-link ops in training-context conversations, the prompt's explicit-forbiddance language was the worst combination (cognitive load + visible signal that primes prohibited operations); (b) Claude had defaulted to the established defense-in-depth pattern without questioning whether the underlying scenario warranted it. Pivoted mid-session to Option A — make sister links truly invisible to the consolidation model (Sister Nodes column dropped from TSV input; sister-link op names never enumerated in prompt; applier rejection retained as silent backstop). Original prompt edits reverted; final implementation is structurally cleaner. **Operational lessons:** (1) before applying an established codebase pattern, ask whether the underlying problem actually fits the pattern's failure-mode profile — defense-in-depth assumes the model benefits from being told what's forbidden, but for AI-as-component scenarios where the model has prior-pattern training, explicit forbiddance may be net-negative; (2) when a Read-It-Back surfaces "why are we doing X at all" rather than "rephrase X" feedback, treat it as an architectural challenge that may warrant scope expansion, not a wording revision; (3) mid-session pivots to "most thorough and reliable" (per `feedback_recommendation_style.md`) are correct even when they expand original LOC estimate — original ~10-20 LOC scope grew to ~115 LOC across 5 files; worth it for the structural correctness gain; (4) the `omitSisterNodesColumn` flag pattern (default-false, additive option on existing serializer) is reusable for future analogous "make this data invisible to a specific call site" scenarios. NOT a critical mistake — the original implementation passed tests and was technically correct against the literal Task #12 spec from the prior STATE block ("drop ADD_SISTER_LINK + REMOVE_SISTER_LINK from consolidation prompt vocabulary listing; tighten consolidationMode in applier"); the director's question reframed the underlying intent more thoroughly than the original deferral capture had. Cross-references: prior session's CORRECTIONS_LOG entry on the original drift surfacing (2026-05-05); `feedback_recommendation_style.md` (most-thorough-and-reliable preference); `feedback_avoid_over_prescribing.md` (similar architectural over-prescription pattern, 2026-05-04 session, three recurrences in one session). Doc updates this session: `AUTO_ANALYZE_CONSOLIDATION_PROMPT_V4.md` Option A rewrite; `KEYWORD_CLUSTERING_ACTIVE.md` new STATE block; `ROADMAP.md` Active Tools row + drift-entry resolution; `CORRECTIONS_LOG.md` (this entry); `CHAT_REGISTRY.md` new top row; `DOCUMENT_MANIFEST.md` timestamps. Code: 5 files modified, +9 net new tests, 336/336 src/lib pass, baseline parity on tsc + build + lint.)
**Last updated in session:** session_2026-05-05-b_sister-link-consolidation-drift-cleanup (Claude Code)
**Previously updated (on `workflow-2-competition-scraping` feature branch):** May 5, 2026 (W#2 doc-reframe session — ONE new entry: INFORMATIONAL — Rule 26 cross-branch TaskList persistence gap. At session start on `workflow-2-competition-scraping`, `TaskList` came back EMPTY despite Tasks #8 + #9 having been registered via `TaskCreate` during `session_2026-05-04_workflow-tool-scaffold-design` on `main` per Rule 26's deferred-items registry pattern. The TaskList state did not persist across the main → workflow-2 branch boundary — TaskList is per-session-scoped, not git-tracked, so registering a task on branch X for execution on branch Y leaves the registry invisible from branch Y. Work was NOT blocked because the director's launch prompt fully specified Tasks #8 + #9's content verbatim; but this exposes a real robustness gap in Rule 26's "TaskList is the canonical externally-observable deferred-items registry" framing when the deferring session's branch and the executing session's branch differ. Operational mitigation (already followed today implicitly): when a deferring session sets up future work on a different branch, the destination-doc-and-section name MUST be embedded in the launch prompt instruction itself per Rule 14e — the deferring session cannot rely on cross-branch TaskList visibility. This was already partially encoded in the `dffd4d9` commit message which named the three docs explicitly + said "must run as the FIRST item of [the next W#2] session" — that prose successfully carried the intent across the branch boundary even though the TaskList didn't. Future architectural improvement option (deferred decision): if cross-branch defer-registry visibility is required, TaskCreate state could be promoted to a git-tracked doc — but that's a methodology-design decision, not a today-fix. Captured here as INFORMATIONAL because (a) work was not blocked, (b) the existing instructions already covered the case via Rule 14e's destination-naming-in-same-sentence, (c) the implicit operational pattern (launch prompt names destinations) is a viable mitigation. NOT a critical failure — just an architectural gap in Rule 26 that future sessions should be aware of when deferring across branches.)

The §A.17 question listed only "long-lived API tokens vs OAuth 2.0 device flow," but the director's free-form brief at §A.15 explicitly says *"there should be a way for the user to enter their credentials to log into the extension"* — pointing to a third option (direct `signInWithPassword`) that wasn't in the question's option set. Surfaced this session by Claude during Cluster 1 review of Q2 — re-reading the brief with fresh eyes flagged the omission. Director chose the previously-missing direct-credentials option (Option A in this session). NOT a bug; not user-caught — process-quality slip in the prior W#2 Workflow Requirements Interview session. Operational lesson: when authoring a "deferred implementation questions" section for a workflow, Claude should explicitly cross-check the option set against the director's free-form brief (if one was provided) before freezing the section. The brief is high-context primary source; the structured Q&A is a derived artifact that can mis-condense. Future Workflow Requirements Interviews (W#3 onward) should add this cross-check as a step before freezing §A.17 (or its equivalent). Captured in `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN.md §B` 2026-05-04 entry. NO impact on the prior session's deliverables — the right option was eventually picked; the cost was one round-trip of explaining the missed option to the director mid-Cluster-1.)

**Previously updated:** May 4, 2026 (Workflow-Tool Scaffold → Components Library design pivot session, third doc-only commit — ONE additional INFORMATIONAL entry on top of the over-prescribing entry: PROCESS-level slip — **incomplete grep-sweep when retiring an architectural vocabulary term.** When this session retired the "Shared Workflow-Tool Scaffold" framing in favor of "Shared Workflow Components Library," the first two commits updated the obvious top-level architectural references (`PLATFORM_REQUIREMENTS.md §12`, `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 20 + 22 + 25 + §4 Step 4b, `PLATFORM_ARCHITECTURE.md` line 477, the `MULTI_WORKFLOW_PROTOCOL.md` reframes, the `CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` updates) but missed the additional in-context references at: `ROADMAP.md` lines 1895/1897/1900/1908/1922/1956/1961/2045 (W#2/3/5 prereqs + Phase 1α section + blocker entry), `KEYWORD_CLUSTERING_ACTIVE.md` lines 3852/4221 (audit-helper + scale-gap references), `PROJECT_CONTEXT.md` line 349 (Workflow Requirements Interview list), `DOCUMENTATION_ARCHITECTURE.md` lines 350/367-371 (interview Q14 + §14.5 section). Director caught the gap by asking "Did you update the main branch to-do items in the roadmap and instructions for Workflow #1?" — a `grep -i scaffold docs/` sweep would have surfaced these in 2 seconds. **Operational lesson:** when an architectural pivot retires a vocabulary term, a comprehensive `grep -i <term> docs/` sweep across all docs is mandatory cleanup BEFORE the doc-only batch commits. The sweep distinguishes architectural references (need updating) from figurative uses (e.g., "narrative scaffolding" — leave alone). All stale architectural refs cleaned up in the third doc-only commit this session. NOT a critical mistake — the canonical docs were correctly updated in commits 1+2; the stale wording in lower-priority docs would have produced confusion in future sessions but not broken anything. Caught mid-session as a 3-error lint regression on `KeywordWorkspace.tsx`; fixed by (a) dropping the synchronous pre-await `setFetchStatus(..., 'loading')` calls (the banner doesn't need that state — only 'retrying' and 'exhausted' which both fire post-await) AND (b) wrapping the useEffect call in an IIFE so the linter's same-file useCallback tracing terminates at the IIFE rather than reaching the post-await setState. Reporting-precision lesson — NOT a mistake; the rule's tracing scope wasn't apparent until the lint output showed it. Operational lesson: any future useEffect that calls a same-file useCallback containing setState should pre-emptively wrap the call in an IIFE. Lint went baseline 16e/41w → 19e/41w → back to 16e/41w after both fixes.)

**Previously updated (claude.ai era):** https://claude.ai/chat/75cc8985-b70a-49f4-8b64-444c34ef541f

**Purpose:** Every mistake made in any chat — whether Claude or user catches it — gets appended. Future Claudes read this to avoid repeating. This is how institutional memory survives Claude's lack of memory.

**Rules:**
- APPEND-ONLY. Never edit or delete past entries.
- Every mistake gets an entry regardless of severity.
- Each entry identifies root cause, not just symptom.
- Each entry includes Prevention describing what was added to prevent recurrence.

---

## Entry format

```
### [YYYY-MM-DD] — [Short mistake description]
**Chat URL:** https://claude.ai/chat/[uuid]
**Tool/Phase affected:** [Name]
**Severity:** Low / Medium / High / Critical

**What happened:** [Description]
**Root cause:** [Why it happened]
**How caught:** [User / Claude / Downstream problem]
**Correction:** [What was changed to fix]
**Prevention:** [What was added to prevent recurrence]
```

---

## Entries

### 2026-05-22-f — P-42 closing entry — `backup-memory-dir.sh` + sibling `track-edited-docs.sh` PostToolUse hooks were silently no-op for three weeks because of a single jq-selector field-name mismatch (`.tool` instead of `.tool_name`); diagnosed empirically via `/tmp/plos-hook-debug.log` stdin capture in one diagnostic step; fix shipped + NEW Layer-3b mirror-staleness SessionStart canary added (INFORMATIONAL — closes the three prior P-42 §Entries; introduces a reusable Pattern for "how to debug a silent PostToolUse hook" + cements the sibling-hook scope expansion as a Rule 24 outcome)

**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL — this entry is the CLOSING §Entry for P-42, not a new slip-of-this-session. The slip itself was captured + tracked across three prior §Entries:

1. **2026-05-20-b initial capture** — `.codespace-backup/memory/` had 10 files vs. source's 12; backup MEMORY.md was stale; manually mirrored via 3 `cp -p` commands at end-of-session.
2. **2026-05-20-c reproduction #2** — wrote new memory file `feedback_handoff_carryovers_to_roadmap.md` + edited MEMORY.md; backup did NOT receive either automatically; manually mirrored via 2 `cp` commands.
3. **2026-05-21 reproduction #3** (paragraph inside the 2026-05-21 Build #8 header batch) — wrote new feedback-memory file `feedback_session_bookends_plain_summary.md` + edited MEMORY.md via Edit; backup mirror was MISSING the new file entirely + the updated MEMORY.md was STALE in the backup (3201 bytes from yesterday vs. source 3515 bytes from today); manually mirrored both via `cp` before the end-of-session commit.

This session diagnosed the root cause empirically + shipped the fix + added a defense-in-depth Layer-3b canary. Captured here informationally (a) so future readers can find the closing narrative under a single dated heading, (b) to memorialize the empirical-instrumentation method as a reusable Pattern, (c) to record the sibling-hook scope expansion as a Rule 24 outcome.

**The headline finding — empirical root-cause confirmation in one diagnostic step.** Added temporary diagnostic instrumentation to `backup-memory-dir.sh` that wrote raw PostToolUse stdin to `/tmp/plos-hook-debug.log` via `tee -a`. Triggered a disposable memory-file Write at `/home/codespace/.claude/projects/-workspaces-brand-operations-hub/memory/_diagnostic_test_p42.md`. The hook fired (proving the hook was wired correctly all along in `.claude/settings.json`) and the captured JSON stdin revealed Claude Code's PostToolUse hook stdin uses the field name **`.tool_name`** — NOT `.tool`. Both hooks were using `jq -r '.tool // empty'`, which always resolved to `""`, so the subsequent Edit/Write gate (`case "$TOOL" in Edit|Write) ...`) always exited without doing work. All three P-42 reproductions stemmed from this single field-name mismatch.

**Fixes shipped this session via build commit `2e3270d` (4 files changed +178/-4):**

1. **`.claude/hooks/backup-memory-dir.sh`** — jq selector `.tool` → `.tool_name`. Hook-contract comment at the top of the file updated to document the empirically-confirmed stdin shape (so the next maintainer doesn't have to re-derive the shape from external docs).
2. **`.claude/hooks/track-edited-docs.sh`** — identical jq selector fix. This is **scope-adjacent to P-42 per Rule 24** — the sibling hook had the identical bug, surfaced during P-42 diagnosis. The `.claude/session-modified-docs.log` had been 0-byte since May 20 because of the same root cause (the doc-batch agent had been falling back to manual reasoning about which docs got touched rather than reading the log). Per Rule 24, when a same-class bug surfaces during diagnosis of another bug, it gets fixed in the same patch + captured informationally — NOT split into a separate ROADMAP item.
3. **NEW `.claude/hooks/check-memory-mirror-staleness.sh`** (~175 LOC) — SessionStart canary for **Layer-3b defense-in-depth**. Compares source memory dir against `.codespace-backup/memory/` mirror on every session start. Emits 🚨 `MEMORY MIRROR STALENESS` alert via `additionalContext` if any of: (a) file count differs; (b) any file present in source is missing from mirror (or vice versa); (c) any per-file size differs beyond a small tolerance. Tested in 3 scenarios: healthy (no alert), source-only file (alert names the missing file), size mismatch (alert names the divergent file + byte counts) — all produced the expected outputs.
4. **`.claude/settings.json`** — wires the new canary into the SessionStart hooks array alongside existing `inject-next-session-pointer.sh` + `check-memory-dir-status.sh`.

**Integration test post-fix** confirmed both layers work end-to-end before commit: wrote disposable `_integration_test_p42.md` to source memory dir → Layer-1 PostToolUse hook fired correctly + mirrored the file to backup → Layer-3b SessionStart canary then reported empty `additionalContext` on the next session-start probe (source + mirror in sync). Diagnostic instrumentation + test files (`_diagnostic_test_p42.md` + `_integration_test_p42.md`) cleaned up post-test in both source + backup before commit.

**Layer architecture after this ship** (Rule 29's 4-layer protective architecture against memory loss, now extended to 5 layers):

- **Layer 1 MECHANICAL** = `backup-memory-dir.sh` PostToolUse hook → fires on every Edit/Write to source memory dir → mirrors to `.codespace-backup/memory/`. **NOW WORKING** after this session's `.tool_name` fix.
- **Layer 2 PROCEDURAL** = HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 29 + `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` — fires before any container-destructive operation; requires Rule 14f forced-picker with backup-first as the recommended option.
- **Layer 3 DETECTION** = `check-memory-dir-status.sh` SessionStart canary for SOURCE-MISSING / SOURCE-EMPTY / SOURCE-PARTIAL-LOSS — catches the case where the source memory dir itself has been wiped.
- **NEW Layer 3b DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH** = `check-memory-mirror-staleness.sh` SessionStart canary for SOURCE-vs-MIRROR DIVERGENCE — catches the case where Layer 1 has regressed OR some other path has left the mirror stale even though source is healthy.
- **Layer 4 RECOVERY** = `scripts/restore-memory-from-backup.sh` — restores source from mirror with safe-mode (refuse if source has unique-not-in-mirror files) + force-mode (override) options.

The 5 layers compose multiplicatively: Layer 1 is the default safety net (auto-mirror on every write); Layer 2 is the procedural gate before any destructive container op; Layer 3 catches source loss at session start; Layer 3b catches silent mirror drift at session start; Layer 4 is the recovery path if Layer 3 or 3b fires. P-42's gap was at Layer 1 — fixed this session — and the new Layer 3b provides defense-in-depth for any future Layer 1 regression.

**Reusable Pattern for "how to debug a silent PostToolUse hook" (informational observation #1).** When a PostToolUse / PreToolUse hook is wired in `.claude/settings.json` but doesn't appear to fire (or fires but does nothing), the canonical diagnostic step is:

1. Add temporary `tee -a /tmp/<hook-name>-debug.log` at the top of the hook script, before any conditional logic, so raw stdin is captured to a known path.
2. Trigger a controlled tool invocation that should fire the hook (e.g., a disposable Write to a path the hook is supposed to act on).
3. Inspect `/tmp/<hook-name>-debug.log` for the actual JSON stdin shape — the field names, the nesting, the tool string, the file_path, the timestamp.
4. Diff the observed shape against what the hook's jq selectors are reading. The mismatch (if any) is usually the root cause.
5. Fix + re-run the controlled invocation + confirm the hook now does the work.
6. Remove the temporary `tee` instrumentation before commit.

This pattern surfaced P-42's root cause in a single diagnostic step — far cheaper than reading Claude Code's hook docs + cross-referencing PostToolUse stdin shape across versions. Worth surfacing as the default first approach for any future hook-not-firing diagnosis. Captured here as an informational Pattern rather than promoted to a HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule because hook diagnosis is rare (typically only when shipping new hook infrastructure or after a Claude Code version bump shifts a field name).

**Sibling-hook shared-bug surfaced via Rule 24 during diagnosis (informational observation #2).** While instrumenting `backup-memory-dir.sh` for the diagnostic capture, noticed that the adjacent `track-edited-docs.sh` had the identical `jq -r '.tool // empty'` pattern. Quick check of `.claude/session-modified-docs.log` showed 0-byte file content since May 20 — confirming the sibling hook had been silently no-op for the same duration as backup-memory-dir.sh. Per Rule 24's pre-capture search guidance ("when a same-class bug surfaces during diagnosis of another bug, fix it in the same patch + capture as scope-adjacent rather than splitting into a separate ROADMAP item"), the sibling fix was included in this commit's scope. Worth noting: the doc-batch agent at session-end had been falling back to manual reasoning about which docs got touched rather than reading the log — that fallback path had been the load-bearing mechanism even though the log was supposed to be load-bearing. Post-fix, future doc-batch agents will have mechanical knowledge of touched docs via the log.

**Canary as Layer-3b defense-in-depth (informational observation #3).** Adding a SessionStart canary is cheaper insurance than fully diagnosing the PostToolUse hook gap — the canary catches divergence regardless of root cause (hook regression / external file edit / cp / mv / manual mirror correction that missed a file / etc.). The most-thorough-and-reliable path per `feedback_recommendation_style.md` is to ship BOTH: fix the Layer 1 root cause AND add Layer 3b defense-in-depth. This session shipped both in one commit. The Layer 3b canary's documentation lives entirely in the hook script's top-of-file comment + this CORRECTIONS_LOG §Entry — no separate design doc needed because the canary's behavior is mechanical + self-evident from reading the script.

**Cross-references:**

- ROADMAP P-42 polish-backlog entry — now flipped to ✅ DONE-AND-VERIFIED 2026-05-22-f with the empirical root-cause confirmation + the scope-adjacent track-edited-docs.sh fix + the new Layer-3b canary; ORIGINAL CAPTURE narrative preserved below the new ✅ status line for traceability.
- CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-05-20-b §Entry "backup-memory-dir.sh PostToolUse hook did NOT auto-fire" — the original slip narrative that became P-42.
- CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-05-20-c §Entry — P-42 reproduction #2.
- CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-05-21 paragraph inside the 2026-05-21 Build #8 header batch — P-42 reproduction #3.
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 29 (Pre-destructive-container-operation audit) — the procedural layer the canary composes with.
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 24 (Pre-capture search) — the rule that justified the scope-adjacent track-edited-docs.sh fix.
- `.claude/hooks/backup-memory-dir.sh` (the fixed Layer-1 hook).
- `.claude/hooks/track-edited-docs.sh` (the fixed sibling hook).
- `.claude/hooks/check-memory-mirror-staleness.sh` (the new Layer-3b canary).
- `.claude/hooks/check-memory-dir-status.sh` (the existing Layer-3 canary; unchanged).
- `.claude/settings.json` (the hook wiring; updated to register the new canary).
- `scripts/restore-memory-from-backup.sh` (the Layer-4 recovery path; unchanged).
- `/workspaces/brand-operations-hub/.codespace-backup/memory/` (the mirror destination — now actually receiving writes).
- P-41 polish-backlog entry (the 4-layer protective architecture this is the Layer-1-fix-plus-Layer-3b-extension of).

**Lesson: hook-script diagnosis is a one-step problem if you instrument stdin capture early.** The temptation to read Claude Code's hook docs + cross-reference field names across versions + guess at the root cause from settings.json wiring is real but wastes time. Capturing the actual stdin shape via `tee` takes 60 seconds + answers the question definitively. Add this to the implicit toolbox for any future "hook isn't firing" or "hook is firing but doing nothing" diagnosis.

**Prevention rule (informational — no new HANDOFF_PROTOCOL rule drafted):** when adding any future PostToolUse / PreToolUse hook, include a self-test in the same commit that triggers the hook + verifies the side effect lands. The 60 seconds of self-test catches the silent-no-op case immediately rather than letting it ride for three weeks.

### 2026-05-20 — Codespaces "Rebuild Container" silently wiped Claude's persistent operational memory directory + the Claude Code CLI binary; recommended by the 2026-05-21 session WITHOUT pre-rebuild audit; recovery + 4-layer protective architecture shipped this session (HIGH-severity — first-of-class slip; the prior-session recommender did not surface that the rebuild would also wipe everything outside `/workspaces/`)

**Severity:** HIGH — distinct from the recurring cwd-leak / wxt-hang / hook-substring-match / API-overload patterns captured prior. This is the FIRST slip where Claude's own persistent operational memory was destroyed AND the slip was not Claude-the-executor's fault but rather Claude-the-prior-session-recommender's fault (the recommendation lacked an audit step). No PLOS or vklf.com work was lost — all PLOS state lives in git + GitHub remote + Vercel + Supabase, all preserved. The loss was Claude's own internal operational memory + dev tooling — meta, not product.

**Context:** the 2026-05-21 session shipped P-18 devcontainer postCreateCommand for Playwright Chromium system libraries (W#2 → main deploy session #30) but deferred real-Chrome verification to the next session because the only path that proves the postCreateCommand wiring actually fires (director's earlier Rule 27 Option A pick = fresh-Codespace-rebuild walkthrough) requires tearing down the running container — which kills Claude's terminal mid-session. The 2026-05-21 NEXT_SESSION.md handoff recommended director perform the rebuild offline between sessions, then bring the PASS/FAIL result to the next Claude session. Director performed the rebuild between 2026-05-21 and 2026-05-20 (today). The rebuild succeeded at P-18's purpose (verification PASS — `npm run test:e2e:all` returned 91/91 in 1.8 min with ZERO manual lib install, conclusive proof the postCreateCommand fired during rebuild + installed all Chromium libs from the canonical README lib list) but **silently wiped everything outside `/workspaces/`**.

**Mistake:** the 2026-05-21 session recommended the rebuild walkthrough WITHOUT auditing what the rebuild would wipe. The recommendation was correct for P-18's purpose (rebuild IS the only way to prove the postCreateCommand fires on a fresh container) but the recommender did not surface to director that the rebuild would also wipe:

- **Claude's persistent operational memory directory** at `/home/codespace/.claude/projects/-workspaces-brand-operations-hub/memory/` (~10 cross-session operational-memory files):
  - `feedback_recommendation_style.md` (director's standing preference for most-thorough/reliable path)
  - `feedback_default_to_recommendation.md` (when director defers, default to the explicit recommendation)
  - `feedback_trust_director_setup_confirmation.md` (don't re-litigate director-confirmed setup)
  - `feedback_playwright_for_repeatable_walkthroughs.md` (operational policy on Playwright as the verification artifact)
  - `feedback_deferred_items_registry.md` (Rule 26 DEFERRED-items handling)
  - `feedback_approval_scope_per_decision_unit.md` (Rule 9 approval-scope vs. push-count semantics)
  - `project_sequential_workflow_operation.md` (the canonical multi-workflow operating model)
  - `project_scaffold_pivot_to_components_library.md` (the standing design directive for shared UI scaffolding)
  - `project_resume_script_design_scheduled.md` (the `./resume` script's standing design + per-workflow pointer architecture)
  - `MEMORY.md` (index of the above)
- **The Claude Code CLI binary itself** — director had to manually reinstall + reauthenticate before this session could start.
- **Global npm packages** (potentially — depends on which were installed outside `/workspaces/`).
- **~/.local/bin/** (potentially).
- **Apt extras** installed beyond the base image.
- **Shell profile edits** in `~/.bashrc` / `~/.zshrc`.
- **VS Code extensions** installed at the Codespace level (not via devcontainer.json).

**How caught:** director caught the slip post-rebuild + this session's SessionStart was the first Claude session after the wipe. The missing memory dir was visible immediately — the `MEMORY.md` index was gone, and the operational files Claude relies on were absent. Director surfaced the slip + requested permanent protective scaffolding.

**Root cause:** at the 2026-05-21 NEXT_SESSION.md write moment, Claude-the-recommender did not perform a pre-rebuild audit of what the rebuild would wipe. The mental model was "rebuild is the test harness for P-18" rather than "rebuild is a destructive container operation that wipes everything outside `/workspaces/`." No procedural rule existed requiring such an audit before recommending destructive container operations.

**Impact:**
- Functional reconstruction of all 10 memory files completed this session from `docs/HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` references; operational rules + cross-references are faithful; any nuance that lived ONLY in the originals is lost.
- ~30 min of session time consumed by reconstruction.
- Director time consumed by manual Claude Code reinstall + reauthentication before session start.
- Zero PLOS or vklf.com impact.

**Correction this session (4-layer protective architecture):**

**MECHANICAL layer** — new `.claude/hooks/backup-memory-dir.sh` PostToolUse hook on Edit + Write. When the target file is inside the memory dir, the hook mirrors it into `/workspaces/brand-operations-hub/.codespace-backup/memory/<basename>` (persistent volume; survives rebuild). Wired into both Edit + Write PostToolUse arrays in `.claude/settings.json`. No Claude memory required; happens automatically.

**PROCEDURAL layer** — new `docs/HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 29 "Pre-destructive-container-operation audit" (substantial section inserted between Rule 28 and §4) defining:
- The trigger list (Codespaces: Rebuild Container, devcontainer.json edits, container reset, manual home-dir wipes).
- The Pre-Container-Audit mechanical checklist (run `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` to enumerate what's outside `/workspaces/` that will be wiped).
- The Rule 14f picker shape (A: backup-first recommended, B: proceed without backup, C: cancel, D: escape hatch).
- The recovery playbook with file list.
- The 4-layer architecture cross-reference.
- Scope exceptions.

Plus new `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` (executable; one-command audit listing what's outside `/workspaces/` — memory dir + global npm packages + ~/.local/bin/ + apt extras + credentials + shell profile + VS Code extensions; output structured for inclusion in the Rule 14f picker). Plus `docs/CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` Rule 8 augment paragraph naming Codespaces rebuild as a destructive container operation requiring the Pre-Container-Audit per Rule 29.

**DETECTION layer** — new `.claude/hooks/check-memory-dir-status.sh` SessionStart hook on matcher=startup. Verifies the memory dir contains the 10 canonical files; emits 🚨 CRITICAL alert as additionalContext if missing/empty/partial-loss. Wired into `.claude/settings.json` SessionStart array alongside the existing inject-next-session-pointer hook.

**RECOVERY layer** — new `scripts/restore-memory-from-backup.sh` (executable; idempotent recovery script with `--force` flag; verifies backup exists, refuses to overwrite populated dest without --force, restores files in sequence, reports success + count, falls through to reconstruction-from-HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md instructions if backup is also missing). Plus new `.codespace-backup/` directory in persistent volume with tracked `README.md` + tracked `.gitkeep` + `memory/` subdirectory containing initial mirror of all 10 reconstructed memory files (gitignored content). `.gitignore` updated: gitignore `.codespace-backup/*` except `README.md` and `.gitkeep`.

**What would have caught this:** Rule 29's Pre-Container-Audit picker. Running `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` BEFORE recommending the rebuild would have surfaced the memory directory + the CLI binary + global npm packages + apt extras as "will be wiped" items; that would have triggered the Rule 14f forced-picker, and director would have chosen the backup-first path (Option A) instead of the proceed-without-backup path that effectively happened by default 2026-05-21.

**Verification this session (the entry IS the test we ran today):**
- SessionStart canary tested with healthy / missing / partial-loss → emits correct alerts.
- PostToolUse backup hook tested with a memory-dir file edit (mirrors correctly into `.codespace-backup/memory/<basename>`) + a non-memory-dir file edit (no-op, as designed).
- Restore script tested in safe-mode (refuses to overwrite) + force-mode (restores all 10 files; diff vs. source = identical).
- `codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` tested in this Codespace → produces structured 7-section report listing memory dir + global npm packages + ~/.local/bin/ + apt extras + credentials + shell profile + VS Code extensions.

**Prevention rule (now formalized as Rule 29):** before recommending OR executing any destructive container operation (Codespaces: Rebuild Container; devcontainer.json edits that require rebuild; container reset; manual home-dir wipes), run `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh`, surface the wipe-list to director via Rule 14f forced-picker (A: backup-first recommended / B: proceed without backup / C: cancel / D: escape hatch), and proceed only on director's explicit pick. The PostToolUse backup hook reduces but does NOT eliminate the need for the picker — the picker exists to give director informed consent BEFORE the destructive op, not just to provide recovery AFTER.

**Slip class for future Claude self-monitoring:** recommend-destructive-container-operation-without-pre-rebuild-audit. Distinct from cwd-leak (executor-side parallel-Bash slip) + wxt-hang (third-party tool quirk) + hook-substring-match (hook precision issue) + API-overload (vendor outage). This class is RECOMMENDER-side: a NEXT_SESSION.md write moment where the writer recommends a destructive op without auditing what it will destroy.

**Cross-references:**
- `docs/HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 29 (the procedural rule + Pre-Container-Audit checklist + Rule 14f picker shape + recovery playbook).
- `docs/CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` Rule 8 (augment paragraph naming Codespaces rebuild as destructive).
- `.claude/hooks/check-memory-dir-status.sh` (SessionStart canary).
- `.claude/hooks/backup-memory-dir.sh` (PostToolUse mirror).
- `scripts/codespace-rebuild-audit.sh` (pre-rebuild audit).
- `scripts/restore-memory-from-backup.sh` (recovery script).
- `.codespace-backup/README.md` (backup-architecture documentation).
- ROADMAP P-18 entry (the verification PASS that surfaced this slip class as a side effect).
- ROADMAP P-41 entry (the protective-scaffolding ship that closes this slip class).
- 2026-05-21 NEXT_SESSION.md offline-steps section (the load-bearing recommend-without-audit moment).

### 2026-05-21 — cwd-leak Bash slip recurred TWICE in one session — pre-deploy scoreboard check 4 (src/lib node:test) + post-merge scoreboard check 6 (Playwright) both bit by the same parallel-batch `cd <subdir>` cwd-leak class (INFORMATIONAL — 6th-or-7th recurrence of the 2026-05-12-c slip class; pattern STRENGTHENING)

**Context:** This session was the W#2 → main deploy session #30 (P-18 devcontainer postCreateCommand ship). Same slip class as 2026-05-16 + 2026-05-15-d + 2026-05-14 + 2026-05-13 + 2026-05-12-c — when a parallel Bash batch contains ANY `cd <subdir>` command, the working directory leaks to subsequent commands in the same batch.

**Mistake 1 (pre-deploy scoreboard, check 4 — src/lib node:test):** ran a parallel Bash batch containing `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npx tsc --noEmit` (check 2) + `find src/lib -name "*.test.ts" ...` (check 4). The `find src/lib` command, intended to run from the repo root, instead ran from `extensions/competition-scraping/` because the `cd` in check 2 had set the parallel batch's effective cwd. Returned **428/428 instead of the expected 536/536** — the extension's test count rather than src/lib's. The mismatch with the baseline (536/536 per 2026-05-20 scoreboard) immediately surfaced the slip.

**Mistake 2 (post-merge scoreboard, check 6 — Playwright):** same root cause in the post-merge scoreboard sequence. Ran a parallel Bash batch with `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm test` (check 3) + `npm run test:e2e:all` (check 6). The Playwright command failed with "Missing script: test:e2e:all" because cwd was still in `extensions/competition-scraping/` from check 3's `cd` — and the extension's `package.json` doesn't define that script (it lives in the root `package.json`).

**Why it happened:** parallel Bash batches share an effective working directory that's set by the most recent `cd` in the batch. The Claude Code Bash tool's working-directory model in parallel calls is per-batch sticky, not per-call independent.

**Recovery:** both caught immediately via output anomaly. Mistake 1: re-ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && find src/lib -name "*.test.ts" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -exec node --test --experimental-strip-types {} +` standalone → got 536/536 confirming the baseline. Mistake 2: re-ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && npm run test:e2e:all` standalone → got 91/91 confirming the post-merge scoreboard baseline. Net cost ~60 seconds total across both instances.

**Prevention rule (already in place since 2026-05-16; this is a strengthening recurrence):** when running parallel Bash calls where ANY command uses `cd`, EVERY command in the batch must explicitly prefix with the desired absolute cwd via `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && <command>` (or whatever the target cwd is). Do NOT rely on the Bash tool defaulting to the repo root between calls in a parallel batch.

**Why this is INFORMATIONAL not real-tier:** the slip class is well-documented; the recovery was fast (~30 sec each); zero production impact. Captured here because the recurrence count is now 6th-or-7th — the pattern STRENGTHENS the prevention rule's load-bearing-ness, not weakens it. Future sessions should treat parallel Bash batches with `cd` calls as a code-smell that requires explicit absolute-cwd prefixes on every command in the batch.

**Cross-references:** 2026-05-16 + 2026-05-15-d + 2026-05-14 + 2026-05-13 + 2026-05-12-c §Entries — same root cause.

---

### 2026-05-21 — Anthropic API 529 Overload storm mid-session — pause-and-resume pattern via NEXT_SESSION.md amendment worked cleanly (NEW operational pattern + Claude-Code-harness quirks observed) (INFORMATIONAL — new operational pattern + two harness quirks worth noting; not actionable in our code)

**Context:** Mid-session today (W#2 → main deploy session #30, P-18 devcontainer ship), after start-of-session reads + drift check + both Rule 14f/Rule 27 pickers + environment-check, RIGHT before the devcontainer file write, Anthropic's API hit a 529 Overloaded storm — repeated `529 Overloaded` errors over a ~30-minute window. Director asked for "a safe way to wrap up this session till Claude has resolved this server issue."

**Pattern executed:** session-pause-via-NEXT_SESSION-amendment.

1. Drafted an amendment to `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` adding a "## 2026-05-21 RESUMPTION NOTE" block at the top of the file (above the pre-existing 2026-05-20-written content), recording:
   - Picker outcomes captured BEFORE the storm (Rule 14f fix-shape Option B = separate `.devcontainer/install-playwright-deps.sh` script; Rule 27 verification Option A = Director walkthrough on a fresh Codespace rebuild).
   - Environment-check results captured BEFORE the storm (Codespace user = `codespace`; OS = Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS Noble Numbat; `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/yarn.list` confirmed present; `.devcontainer/` directory does NOT exist today; `npm run test:e2e:all` resolves to `playwright test`; README's full canonical lib list at lines 50-74; Playwright's `--with-deps` ruled out per README's yarn.list GPG issue).
   - Explicit instruction to the next session: "skip re-firing the pickers; proceed straight to Option B + Option A coding."

2. Commit `1e28bd1` "Pause P-18 session — encode picker outcomes in NEXT_SESSION.md (Anthropic API 529 storm)" — deliberately did NOT contain the keyword "End-of-session" in the message subject so the `check-next-session-doc.sh` PreToolUse hook wouldn't fire (the hook is scoped to actual end-of-session commits, not session-pauses). **First commit attempt hit the hook** because the message body contained the phrase "End-of-session" in negation context ("This is a session-pause amendment, NOT a closing doc batch"). Mechanical fix was to rephrase the negation phrasing in the message body to avoid the substring; cleaner long-term fix would be for the hook to match the FULL phrase "End-of-session doc batch" (the canonical commit-message prefix) rather than the looser "End-of-session" substring. Captured for future hook refinement.

3. Pushed `1e28bd1` to `origin/workflow-2-competition-scraping` only (NOT main — no Rule 9 trigger since main wasn't moved). Branch state: workflow-2 ahead of main by 1 commit (the amendment).

4. Produced a Personalized Handoff for the director describing the pause + the expected resume flow.

5. Director said "Please pickup where we encountered the 529 overload errors" when the storm cleared ~30 minutes later.

6. Resumed by reading the RESUMPTION NOTE + jumping straight to the devcontainer file writes — zero picker re-litigation, zero environment-check re-run. ~5 min of session time + analysis-quality of the picker decisions preserved.

**Why the pattern worked:**
- The pause-amendment captured everything that lived ONLY in the chat transcript (picker outcomes + environment-check). Without this, a fresh session would have had to re-do both.
- The amendment was committed + pushed, so the state was durable even if the Codespace died during the outage.
- The commit was scoped to workflow-2 (not main), so it didn't trigger Rule 9 deploy gates or affect main's state.

**Why this is INFORMATIONAL not real-tier:** the pattern is a new operational artifact, not a slip. Captured here so future sessions encountering API outages have a documented playbook: (1) amend NEXT_SESSION.md with picker outcomes + work-completed-before-the-storm that lives only in chat transcript; (2) commit + push to feature branch only (NOT main); (3) produce a Personalized Handoff for the director; (4) wait for storm to clear; (5) resume from the RESUMPTION NOTE.

**Bonus observation #1 — cp/pkill SIGUSR1 race during the deploy's extension-zip rename (Claude-Code-harness quirk):** the `pkill -f "wxt zip" && cp .output/competition-scraping-extension-0.1.0-chrome.zip /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/plos-extension-2026-05-21-w2-deploy-30.zip && ls -la /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/plos-extension-*.zip` chain exit-coded 144 (SIGUSR1 = 128 + 16) due to a SIGCHLD/SIGUSR1 race with a background-task completion notification firing at exactly the moment the wxt-zip parent completed. Retried the `cp` standalone, succeeded. Second time this session a background-task completion notification has interfered with foreground Bash execution. Not actionable in our code (no fix needed); informational evidence that the Claude-Code-harness has a small race condition around background-task completion notifications interrupting foreground shells.

**Bonus observation #2 — `check-next-session-doc.sh` PreToolUse hook substring matching:** as noted above, the hook matched on the loose substring `End-of-session` even in negation contexts inside the commit message body. The hook's intent is to catch actual end-of-session commits and gate them on NEXT_SESSION.md being staged. Cleaner long-term fix would be to match the full canonical commit-message prefix `End-of-session doc batch` (the format every real end-of-session commit uses) rather than the looser `End-of-session` substring. Captured here as a future hook-refinement suggestion; not blocking; the workaround (rephrase the negation to avoid the substring) worked for this session.

**Cross-references:** `docs/HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 28 (multi-layered defense for resume flow); `feedback_session_management.md` (wrap before degrading); `feedback_approval_scope_per_decision_unit.md` (the pause-amendment push is a workflow-only push, NOT a Rule 9 deploy push, so director's "deploy" approval scope doesn't apply).

---

### 2026-05-19-d-2 — §4 Step 1c forced-picker omitted "ship-remaining-polish-first" option — picker presupposed Tool Graduation was the natural next step on the premise that P-16 was "the last open W#2 polish item with concrete code-level work" when 6 open polish items remained in the backlog (REAL-TIER — option-set narrowing slip; new prevention rule)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-19-d_w2-main-deploy-session-25-p16-sw-mv3-crash-diagnostics-DEPLOYED` (Claude Code, dual-branch).

**What happened.** At end of session 2026-05-19-d, after P-16 SW MV3 crash diagnostics was shipped + deployed + wiring-verified on real Chrome, I (Claude) fired the §4 Step 1c forced-picker for the next session's focus per HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 14f. The picker offered three options:

- (A) W#2 Tool Graduation per HANDOFF_PROTOCOL §4 Step 2 Scenario B on `main` — **recommended** per `feedback_recommendation_style.md` (most thorough/reliable).
- (B) W#3 Therapeutic Strategy first session per Rule 18.
- (C) W#1 graduated-tool re-entry per Rule 22.

Director picked (A) Tool Graduation. I then ran the full corrective doc-batch + handoff message presenting Tool Graduation as the next session. Director caught the slip post-handoff after asking *"How many sessions are there remaining for Workflow #2?"* — I honestly answered "1 scheduled session (Tool Graduation), 0 mandatory after that, ~5-6 polish items that COULD become sessions if you choose to ship them but aren't scheduled and might never happen." Director's framing: *"All these things should ship before Workflow #2 is deemed complete."*

That made it clear: the picker should have included a fourth option — **(D) ship the 6 remaining open W#2 polish items (P-18 + P-22 + P-23 + P-24 + P-25 + P-26) BEFORE graduating** — and (D) is what was actually most-thorough/reliable per `feedback_recommendation_style.md`, not (A). Estimated ~7-12 more polish sessions before graduation now.

**Root cause.** Two compounding factors:

1. **Mis-read of HANDOFF_PROTOCOL §4 Step 2 Scenario B step 6.** Step 6 reads: *"Move outgoing tool's outstanding polish items into `<W_OUT>_POLISH_BACKLOG.md` (a thin sidecar — so the polish list doesn't lock the tool in active state)."* I read this as "open polish items at graduation time is a valid + expected state — they go to the sidecar" and concluded graduation was natural even with 6 open items. The protocol DOES allow this technically — but the director's framing of "complete" is stronger than the protocol's framing of "graduable." The sidecar pattern is for items that genuinely won't ship (open questions / deferred large lifts / abandoned ideas), NOT for the active polish queue.

2. **`feedback_recommendation_style.md` mis-application.** The standing recommendation rule is "most thorough/reliable" — but in the §4 Step 1c picker I scored "Tool Graduation" as more-thorough than alternatives without considering that the most-thorough path was actually "ship the polish items first so graduation captures a genuinely-complete workflow." Adding more polish work to the queue feels less-thorough at the meta-level (more total sessions = more total work), but it's MORE thorough at the workflow-completion level (a graduated workflow with 6 unshipped polish items is structurally less complete than one with all items shipped).

**Prevention rule (new — addition to `feedback_recommendation_style.md` operational scope + §4 Step 1c interview shape):**

> **When firing the §4 Step 1c forced-picker AND the outgoing workflow has OPEN items in its polish backlog, the picker MUST include an explicit option to "ship the open polish items first, THEN graduate."** This option must be considered for the (recommended) marker per `feedback_recommendation_style.md` because completing the polish queue before graduation is MORE thorough than graduating with open items moving to sidecar. The sidecar pattern is for items that genuinely won't ship (deferred-large-lift / abandoned-design / open-questions-awaiting-director-answer), NOT for the active polish queue. Default to recommending "ship-first" when the backlog has 2+ open items at non-LOW severity; recommend graduation only when the backlog is empty OR contains exclusively items the director has explicitly tagged as "won't ship" / "deferred-large-lift" / "open-question."

**Mechanical test before sending a §4 Step 1c picker:**

1. Grep the workflow's polish backlog for OPEN items.
2. Count OPEN items with severity ≥ MEDIUM.
3. If count ≥ 2: include "ship-remaining-polish-first" as an explicit picker option AND consider it for the (recommended) marker.
4. If count = 0-1 OR all items are LOW + deferred-large-lift: graduation is acceptable as recommended.

**Recovery cost.** Corrective doc-batch — one extra commit cycle (this addendum + ROADMAP (a.47) rewrite + CHAT_REGISTRY amendment + NEXT_SESSION full rewrite + DOCUMENT_MANIFEST amendment) + one extra push + ping-pong. ~10-15 minutes of director's time + ~10 minutes of my time. Real cost: the original handoff message painted W#2 as "functionally complete" which would have framed the next session wrongly if not caught immediately. Director catching it post-handoff (before the next session started) is the failure mode this prevention rule blocks for the future.

**Cross-references:** `feedback_recommendation_style.md` (operational memory governing recommendation style); HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md Rule 14f (forced-picker mechanism) + Rule 26 (deferred-items registry); HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md §4 Step 1c "No obvious next task" interview (where this picker fires); HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md §4 Step 2 Scenario B step 6 (the sidecar pattern that was mis-applied as license for graduation-with-open-items). Commit `be70d86` (the original doc batch with the wrong (a.47) — preserved per Rule 8 commit-history-frozen); this addendum commit on top.

---

### 2026-05-19-c-3 — Wrong-feature-shipped-twice slip — misread director's natural-use feedback as a generic "auto-select something" feature request and shipped P-38 + P-39 before P-40 finally landed the correct fix (REAL-TIER — recurrence-pattern slip with prevention rule)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-19-c_w2-main-deploy-sessions-21-22-23-24-p13-then-p38-p39-reverted-p40-shipped` (Claude Code, dual-branch).
**Tool/Phase affected:** W#2 polish-backlog scope-add via mid-session Rule 11 forced-picker; specifically my interpretation of director's natural-use feedback during P-13 verification.
**Severity:** REAL-TIER. ~2 hours of director time consumed by two wrong-feature ships + verification rounds + the final correction cycle. P-38 + P-39 reverted via `git revert` (no production fallout — both worked correctly for what they did, just solved the wrong problem). The session's lucidity budget burned past the point I should have wrapped per `feedback_session_management.md`.

**What happened.** During P-13 verification, director surfaced natural-use feedback: *"popup did not autoselect the url for 'Attach to which saved URL?'"*. I interpreted this as a generic "auto-select something" feature request and ran a Rule 14f forced-picker with three options:
- (A) Defer + capture as new polish item (marked Recommended by me — slip #1: per `feedback_recommendation_style.md` the marker should have gone on a more-thorough option that actually closed the friction)
- (B) Fix in this session with simple 1-URL pre-select rule
- (C) Fix in this session with sticky most-recently-used preference (framed as "more thorough")

Director picked (B). I shipped P-38 (1-URL pre-select). Director's verification first appeared to fail; investigation showed their "1-URL" test combo actually had 2+ URLs. I had to ask director to manufacture a synthetic 1-URL test scenario (add one URL on a previously-unused platform) to verify the fix. P-38 PASS on synthetic test.

Director then pushed back: their real-world combos have 2+ URLs so P-38 doesn't help. I ran a SECOND scope-expansion picker offering (a) defer (Recommended by me — slip #2: same speed-bias as slip #1), (b) ship simple 1-URL rule (already done), (c) ship sticky preference (more thorough). Director picked the "fix it now" option for sticky preference. I shipped P-39. Director's verification of P-39 also required a synthetic test setup (multi-URL combo with prior session-pick to seed the sticky preference). P-39 PASS on synthetic test.

Director's third pushback: *"All of these tests passed but this was not the issue we were trying to fix. The 'Pick a saved url' dropdown should be prechosen if the url page is already added to vklf.com (which isn't the case currently)."* That's when I realized "auto-select the URL" meant **the URL matching the page currently open**, not a generic auto-select-something rule. The right-click content-script overlay had been doing this since P-15 + P-21 (`pickInitialUrl` + platform-module `canonicalProductUrl`). The popup just needed the analog using `chrome.tabs.query`.

**Consequence (what I shipped + reverted).** Two wrong-feature deploy cycles (#22 + #23) before the third attempt (#24) landed correctly. Net code churn: P-38 commit `a0d5c8a` (4 LOC) + P-39 commit `2766031` (38 LOC across 3 files including 13 node:test cases) → reverted via `git revert` commits `b635ae7` + `f0cef37`. P-40 commit `182da37` (15 LOC reusing existing helpers) shipped + verified PASS on real workflow in deploy session #24.

**Root cause.** I jumped from a one-line natural-use feedback ("popup did not autoselect the url") to drafting Rule 14f picker options without first asking director the specific behavior they wanted. The natural-language ambiguity in "the url" (THE url for what? for the page they're on? for the user's last save? for the only URL if there's one?) needed a Rule 14a Read-It-Back BEFORE drafting picker options, not after. Per HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 14a: *"Before asking the user any question, Claude must mentally read the question back as if it were the user reading it for the first time."* I applied this to the picker options I drafted, not to the SOURCE of the picker (the director's original feedback). The picker options were jargon-free + had context; what they failed at was being about the WRONG FEATURE.

**Specific signals I missed both times before the third attempt.** When asking director to manufacture a synthetic test scenario to verify a fix is required, that's a tell the fix doesn't address their real workflow. Twice in this session I had director manufacture synthetic setups — once for P-38 (fresh 1-URL combo on a previously-unused platform), once for P-39 (multi-URL combo with prior session-pick to seed the sticky preference). Neither setup matched their daily workflow. I missed the signal both times.

A second signal: director's mid-session frustration message ("You only made me test platforms where there is only one url added. Why didn't you bother fixing the actual issue...") was framed as a question but functioned as feedback that I had been solving the wrong problem all along. I acknowledged the recommendation-marker slip (Rule 14f) but did NOT reframe the picker options to surface "active-tab-URL-match" as a new option. I should have at that point pushed back the picker with the director's actual intent named, not just shipped P-39 with a Recommended marker corrected.

**Correction.** Reverted P-38 + P-39 via `git revert` (preserves git history per Rule 8 — never rewrite history). Shipped P-40 with the active-tab-URL-match pattern reusing `pickInitialUrl` + platform-module `canonicalProductUrl` from the content-script overlay's existing P-15 + P-21 work. P-40 verified PASS on director's real workflow (open Chrome on a saved competitor product page → click extension icon → popup pre-selects that page's saved URL automatically). Deploy session #24's main push carried all three commits (2 reverts + P-40).

**Prevention.** Mechanical Rule 14a extension applied at the moment of drafting Rule 14f picker options for a mid-session scope-add: when director surfaces feedback with natural-language ambiguity, FIRST ask director to clarify the specific behavior intended BEFORE drafting picker options. The clarification question should itself follow Rule 14b (situation + options as user-visible differences + recommendation with reasoning) — but applied to the FEATURE DEFINITION, not to the implementation approach. Concrete shape:

> *"When you say 'the popup didn't auto-select the URL', I want to make sure I understand which URL you mean. Three possibilities — pick one or tell me a fourth:*
> *(A) THE URL matching the page you're currently on (i.e., open extension on a saved Amazon page → that page's URL pre-selected) — same pattern as the right-click overlay does today via pickInitialUrl.*
> *(B) Some default URL like the most-recently-saved one across all your popup uses (sticky preference).*
> *(C) The only URL if there happens to be exactly one saved for this Project + Platform combo.*
> *(D) Different — let me explain."*

Had this picker fired at the moment director surfaced the feedback, they would have picked (A) and we would have skipped directly to P-40 without the P-38 + P-39 detour. ~2 hours saved.

**Operational test for future sessions.** When a mid-session natural-use feedback arrives, BEFORE drafting Rule 14f picker options for the fix:
1. Pause + draft a feature-definition Read-It-Back picker (per the shape above).
2. Each option is a possible interpretation of the feedback, not a possible implementation.
3. If the director's intent is obvious from context (e.g., they literally said "match the page I'm on"), you can skip the picker — but the default is to fire it.
4. Once director picks the correct interpretation, THEN run the implementation-shape Rule 14f picker (Option A/B/C/D for HOW to fix it).

**Recurrence class.** Same root cause class as the 2026-04-27 entry (proposed context-scaling ROADMAP item without grep) and the 2026-05-17 entry (doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status). All three share: jumping from a one-line input → drafting decision options without first verifying the input. Rule 24 (pre-capture search) is the canonical scaffolding for one variant (verify against docs/code before adding ROADMAP items); this entry extends the scaffolding to "verify against director's intent before drafting picker options for a mid-session scope-add."

**Cross-references:**
- `session_2026-05-19-c_w2-main-deploy-sessions-21-22-23-24-p13-then-p38-p39-reverted-p40-shipped` (this session).
- Commit `a0d5c8a` (P-38 ship — reverted).
- Commit `2766031` (P-39 ship — reverted).
- Commit `b635ae7` (revert P-39).
- Commit `f0cef37` (revert P-38).
- Commit `182da37` (P-40 ship — the actually-correct fix).
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 14a (Read-It-Back test) + Rule 14b (required structure for user-decision questions) + Rule 24 (pre-capture search; this entry extends the principle to mid-session scope-add picker drafting).
- 2026-04-27 §Entry (original Rule 24 slip — same root cause class) + 2026-05-17 §Entry (doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status — same root cause class).
- `feedback_recommendation_style.md` (most thorough/reliable — also slipped on twice in this session via Rule 14f recommendation-marker on speed-biased options; partially captured in the 2026-05-19-c-2 numbering-collision §Entry; the deeper pattern is captured here).

### 2026-05-19-c — ROADMAP P-13 "Where" drift — popup vs. overlay scope misframed at original P-13 capture time 2026-05-12 (INFORMATIONAL — no production impact)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-19-c_w2-main-deploy-sessions-21-22-p13-p27-paired-DEPLOYED` (Claude Code, dual-branch).
**Tool/Phase affected:** W#2 polish-backlog capture mechanism — specifically the P-13 polish-backlog entry at `ROADMAP.md:107`.
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL (Low). No production impact; drift was caught at start-of-session per Rule 3 BEFORE any code work; ~5 minutes consumed by the drift-check + scope-reduction confirmation; net result was a CLEANER ship (1 LOC × 1 file instead of "~5 LOC × 2-3 files" as planned).

**What happened.** The launch prompt for `session_2026-05-19-c` said *"Ship P-13 — LOW autofocus on '+ Add new…' inline category input"* with a Pre-build read list naming two files: `extensions/competition-scraping/src/entrypoints/popup/components/CapturedTextPasteForm.tsx` (popup React) AND `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/text-capture-form.ts` (content-script overlay). The launch prompt anticipated possibly a third file (`image-capture-form.ts`) via grep-at-session-start check. This framing came from `ROADMAP.md:107` P-13 entry which named the same two files in its **Where:** field.

Code verification at session start (mandatory grep across all candidate files BEFORE coding):

```
$ grep -n "newCategoryInput.focus\|\.focus()" extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/{text-capture-form,image-capture-form,url-add-form}.ts
extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/text-capture-form.ts:305:    newCategoryInput.focus();
extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/image-capture-form.ts:368:      newCategoryInput.focus();
extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/url-add-form.ts:244:        setTimeout(() => newInput.focus(), 0);
```

All 3 content-script overlays ALREADY HAD `.focus()` calls. The ROADMAP P-13 entry's "Where" field was wrong about `text-capture-form.ts` needing the fix (it didn't — the fix was already in the code). Only the popup React component (`CapturedTextPasteForm.tsx:262`) lacked autofocus.

**Consequence (avoided).** Had the work proceeded as written:
- I would have added `inputRef.current?.focus()` via `useEffect` (or equivalent) to `text-capture-form.ts` near line 305, which would have been **redundant** with the already-shipped `newCategoryInput.focus()` call on that same line.
- The "redundant focus" wouldn't have caused user-visible bugs (focus() is idempotent), but it would have polluted the diff with no-op code.
- More worryingly: the launch prompt's "IF YES, fix in 3 files instead of 2" hedge on `image-capture-form.ts` would have led me to also touch THAT file, again adding redundant no-op code.

Rule 3 (code wins over doc) protected the session from acting on the false premise. The actual P-13 ship reduced from "~5 LOC × 2-3 files" to **1 LOC × 1 file** — single `autoFocus` prop on the conditionally-rendered `<input>` in `CapturedTextPasteForm.tsx:262`. The 3 content-script overlays were correct already; only the React popup needed the fix.

**Root cause.** The P-13 entry was originally captured 2026-05-12 in the verification session that surfaced six DEFERRED polish items (P-12 through P-17). The capture's **Where:** field listed BOTH popup + content-script-overlay surfaces because S4-A (where the director reported the missing autofocus) was the popup paste-text walkthrough — but the deferred-items registry didn't differentiate "this surface needs the fix" from "this surface has the same affordance." The original capture's grep against the 4 files (Rule 24 sub-step d) would have surfaced the existing `.focus()` calls in the 3 overlays and reduced the captured scope to popup-only at flag time — but didn't fire. Same root cause class as the 2026-05-17 doc-vs-code drift entry (P-27 back-end status; polish-backlog capture missed code-state verification).

**How caught.** Today's session-start mandatory grep against all 4 candidate files showed `text-capture-form.ts:305`, `image-capture-form.ts:368`, and `url-add-form.ts:244` already had `.focus()` calls. Surfaced to director as part of the drift-check BEFORE any code edit; director agreed scope reduces to popup-only; ROADMAP entry corrected in this end-of-session doc batch.

**Correction.** P-13 entry in `ROADMAP.md:107` had its **Where:** field updated to popup-only (`CapturedTextPasteForm.tsx`); the 3 content-script overlays explicitly noted as ALREADY HAS `.focus()` calls per `text-capture-form.ts:305` + `image-capture-form.ts:368` + `url-add-form.ts:244`. Entry flipped Status: ✅ DONE 2026-05-19-c via commit `e217eb9`. The "fix shape ~2 LOC per file × 2-3 files" claim in the original entry replaced with "1 LOC `autoFocus` prop on popup React `<input>`."

**Prevention.** When a polish-backlog entry names specific files as needing a fix, the pre-coding session MUST grep the actual fix-pattern (here `.focus()`) against ALL named files + adjacent files of the same affordance class BEFORE starting code work. The grep result IS the start-of-session drift check for that polish item. Same shape as the 2026-05-17 doc-vs-code drift entry's prevention rule — Rule 24 sub-step d (verify against actual code) MUST fire at THREE moments in a polish item's lifecycle: (1) at original capture time (when adding the entry), (2) at start of the ship session (mandatory drift check before coding), (3) at start of any verify-and-close session that revisits the item. Today's session fired the check at moment (2) and caught what moment (1) missed.

**Mechanical test:** before starting code work on any polish item that has a "Where:" file list, run `grep -n "<fix-pattern>" <all named files>` and surface ANY hits to the director with a one-line "scope-reduce?" question. Even if the hits are "wrong location" (different function, comment-out, etc.), surfacing them is cheap and prevents redundant ship work. Net cost ~30 seconds; benefit can be entire-session-scope reduction as today proved.

**Cross-references:**
- `docs/ROADMAP.md:107` (the corrected P-13 entry after this session's doc batch).
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/text-capture-form.ts:302-310` (the `categorySelect.addEventListener('change', ...)` handler that already calls `newCategoryInput.focus()` on sentinel pick).
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/image-capture-form.ts:368` (same affordance, same pre-existing fix).
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/url-add-form.ts:244` (same affordance with `setTimeout(..., 0)` defer pattern).
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/entrypoints/popup/components/CapturedTextPasteForm.tsx:262` (the popup React `<input>` that DID need + got the `autoFocus` prop in commit `e217eb9`).
- 2026-05-17 §Entry (doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status; same root cause class — polish-backlog capture missed code-state verification at flag time).
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 3 (code wins over doc) + Rule 24 sub-step d (verify against actual code).

### 2026-05-19-c-2 — Polish-item numbering collision — today's commits labeled "P-27" / "P-28" but those numbers were already taken by existing deployed polish items (INFORMATIONAL — no production impact)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-19-c_w2-main-deploy-sessions-21-22-p13-p27-paired-DEPLOYED` (Claude Code, dual-branch).
**Tool/Phase affected:** W#2 polish-backlog numbering — specifically commit messages on `e217eb9` (no impact; correctly references P-13) + `a0d5c8a` (mislabeled "P-27").
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL (Low). No production impact; the polish items themselves shipped correctly (functionality is fine); only the numbering label was wrong. ~10 minutes consumed mid-doc-batch to re-align the doc layer to canonical numbering.

**What happened.** Mid-session, when the director surfaced the popup-URL-pre-select request as a Rule 11 scope-add to today's P-13 session, I labeled the new polish item "P-27" in the commit message of `a0d5c8a` and in all subsequent doc-batch references. Without first grepping `ROADMAP.md` for existing `P-NN` numbers in use. Similarly, when the director picked the sticky-URL-preference DEFERRED item to defer to a future session, I labeled it "P-28" in the TaskCreate description + initial doc-batch references.

End-of-session doc-batch verification grep (`grep -hoE "P-[0-9]+" docs/ROADMAP.md docs/COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md | sort -t- -k2 -n -u`) showed:
- P-12 through P-37 already in use (highest existing = P-37).
- **Existing P-27:** delete captured texts and images from URL detail page (deployed 2026-05-18 in W#2 → main deploy session #17; commit `f58ca8c` ff-merged to main).
- **Existing P-28:** delete saved URLs with cascade disclosure (also deployed 2026-05-18 in same deploy session #17; same commit).

Both existing items had been live on vklf.com for one day at the moment of today's commits. The collision was 100% avoidable via a single grep at the moment of naming.

**Consequence (avoided).** The collision was caught at end-of-session before the canonical ROADMAP + COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG entries were written. Had it not been caught:
- ROADMAP would have two "P-27" entries (existing delete-text/images + my popup-pre-select) and two "P-28" entries (existing delete-URLs + my deferred sticky-preference) — destroying the canonical numbering convention.
- VERIFICATION_BACKLOG would have the same collision in two sections.
- Future polish-backlog searches via grep `P-27` would return ambiguous hits.

**Correction.** Director picked Option A (renumber to P-38 + P-39) via in-session forced-picker. End-of-session doc-batch updates corrected the renumber in: NEXT_SESSION.md (12 references), CHAT_REGISTRY.md line 5 (my new entry, 19 references), DOCUMENT_MANIFEST.md line 4 (my new entry, 12 references), CORRECTIONS_LOG.md line 5 (my new header, 6 references — see this entry + the P-13 "Where" entry above for the renumber). TaskCreate #14 description renamed from "P-28" → "P-39". Forthcoming ROADMAP + VERIFICATION_BACKLOG + COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN entries (still to write in this same doc batch) use P-38 + P-39 directly. **Git history of commits `e217eb9` (P-13 — correct) and `a0d5c8a` (says "P-27" — mislabel) is FROZEN per CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER Rule 8 (never rewrite git history).** Future references should look up the canonical polish-item number in ROADMAP, not in the commit message of `a0d5c8a`. Cross-references added to the new P-38 ROADMAP entry + this CORRECTIONS_LOG entry so the discrepancy is permanently navigable.

**Root cause.** Same root cause class as today's earlier P-13 "Where" drift §Entry — Rule 24 pre-capture search (sub-step a: direct keyword grep) was not run at the moment of naming. The Rule 24 rule's spirit is "verify before writing"; my Rule 11 scope-add flow at mid-session jumped from "director picked the simple rule" → "write the commit message" without a single grep for existing P-NN. Same class of slip is captured in CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-04-27 (the original Rule 24 entry — Claude proposed a context-scaling ROADMAP item without first searching), 2026-05-17 (doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status — polish-backlog capture missed code-state verification), and today's earlier P-13 "Where" §Entry. Pattern is recurring; the mechanical prevention rules accumulate but aren't fired automatically — Claude needs to fire them on its own at every relevant moment.

**Prevention.** **Mechanical test at every moment a new `P-NN` polish-item number is about to be assigned (in commit message, TaskCreate, doc reference, anywhere):** `grep -hoE "P-[0-9]+" docs/ROADMAP.md docs/COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md | sort -t- -k2 -n -u | tail -1` — this returns the highest existing polish-item number. The next available number is `+1`. Cost: 1 grep, <2 seconds. Anti-pattern caught: assigning sequential-looking numbers (P-27 next after P-26 in director's mental model) without checking that P-27 isn't taken by an earlier session's deployed work.

This is a Rule 24 extension applied to polish-item numbering at name-assignment time — analogous to Rule 24's existing scope (ROADMAP item capture) and the 2026-05-19 NEXT_SESSION.md pointer-write extension. The grep is the same shape; just the input field differs.

**Cross-references:**
- Commit `a0d5c8a` ("P-27" mislabel in message; canonical polish-item number is P-38).
- Commit `e217eb9` (correctly references P-13; no mislabel).
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` W#2 polish backlog: new P-38 entry (✅ DONE; cross-references commit `a0d5c8a`'s mislabel) + new P-39 entry (⏳ OPEN, deferred to next session) — both forthcoming in this same doc batch.
- `docs/COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md`: new P-38 entry + new P-39 entry — forthcoming in this same doc batch.
- 2026-05-19-c §Entry above ("ROADMAP P-13 'Where' drift") — same Rule 24 sub-step-d slip class, different surface.
- 2026-05-17 §Entry (doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status) — same Rule 24 sub-step-a slip class, different surface.
- 2026-04-27 §Entry (the original Rule 24 entry — Claude proposed context-scaling ROADMAP item without first searching) — the canonical earliest instance of this slip class.
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 24 sub-step a (direct keyword grep) + Rule 8 (never rewrite git history).
- CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER Rule 8 (NEVER run destructive git commands).
