### 2026-05-19 — Doc-drift on (a.42) / (a.13) — yesterday's NEXT_SESSION.md framed P-17 as "still pending" despite ROADMAP already marking it ✅ SHIPPED 2026-05-14 (INFORMATIONAL — no production impact)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-19_p17-verify-and-close` (Claude Code, on `main`).
**Tool/Phase affected:** session-handoff pipeline — specifically the `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` write step at end of `session_2026-05-18-c`.
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL (Low). No production impact; today's session caught the drift at start-of-session per Rule 3 before any duplicate test code was written; ~10 minutes consumed by the verification + drift-check + this entry. Zero false work shipped.

**What happened.** The launch prompt I was given at start of `session_2026-05-19` said *"Ship P-17 — real-fetch integration test for `src/lib/authFetch.ts` production export… per ROADMAP Active Tools W#2 row (a.13)."* Yesterday's `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` (written by `session_2026-05-18-c` at the §4 Step 1c forced-picker that fired at end-of-session) framed `(a.42) RECOMMENDED-NEXT = (a.13) P-17` as still pending, with a Pre-build read list that included a Rule 14f forced-picker between jsdom-in-vitest / Playwright / Component-Testing for today's session-start.

In fact, P-17 had already shipped on 2026-05-14 (commit `422c658`, seventy-ninth Claude Code session). Evidence on `main` at session start: `tests/playwright/authFetch-regression.spec.ts` exists with 2 tests (200 path + 401-refresh-retry path), both asserting `threwIllegalInvocation: false`, sanity-checked in-session 2026-05-14 by reverting `authFetch.ts:84-88` to bare-`fetch` and confirming both tests fail LOUDLY. `docs/ROADMAP.md` line 113 says **Status: ✅ SHIPPED at code level 2026-05-14**. `playwright.config.ts:5–9` explicitly comments *"The P-17 authFetch real-browser regression test (2026-05-14)…"*. Yesterday's deploy session #19 already confirmed Playwright 75/75 GREEN on `main`.

**Consequence (avoided).** Had the misframed task been executed as written, today's session would have built a duplicate Playwright suite (~50–100 LOC) + new bundle entrypoint + new stub page, almost certainly with name collisions against the existing `authFetch-regression.spec.ts`. Rule 3 (code wins over doc) protected the session from acting on the false premise.

**Root cause.** Yesterday's §4 Step 1c forced-picker offered "(a.42) = (a.13) P-17 authFetch real-fetch integration test on `main`" as Option A. When the director picked that option, the pointer-author (Claude in `session_2026-05-18-c`) drafted the next-session launch prompt around the candidate **without verifying that (a.13) was still open in ROADMAP at that moment**. The Pre-build read list's *"`tests/playwright/authFetch-regression.spec.ts` (if it exists from P-17 prior work; check via `ls`)"* hedge was the exact spot where uncertainty should have surfaced — but the pointer-author treated the hedge as a "the next session can check" handoff rather than as "I should grep now." Rule 24 (Pre-capture search) is the rule whose spirit was violated: a pointer-file write at end-of-session is functionally equivalent to a forward-looking ROADMAP capture in that it commits a specific item to the next session's queue.

**How caught.** Today's session, mandatory start-of-session step 6 (`git log --oneline -10`) showed commit `422c658` ("P-17 SHIPPED") on main's recent history. The Pre-build read list's `ls tests/playwright/` surfaced `authFetch-regression.spec.ts` already on disk. Reading the file confirmed it IS the P-17 test. Reading ROADMAP line 113 confirmed (a.13) is ✅ SHIPPED. Reading ROADMAP "Previously updated: May 14, 2026" confirmed the full P-17 ship session narrative. Surfaced to director as a drift-check + Rule 14f forced-picker (Verify-and-close / Extend-suite / Pivot / Question-first) BEFORE any work was done. Director picked Verify-and-close (recommended).

**Correction.** Re-ran the existing P-17 Playwright suite on today's main — 2/2 PASS in 3.0s. Re-ran the 7 `authFetch` unit tests — 7/7 PASS. Tsc clean. ROADMAP gets a new (a.42) row marked ✅ DONE-as-already-shipped-and-re-verified pointing back at (a.13). Today's NEXT_SESSION.md (written at end-of-session) rewrites the pointer with a fresh §4 Step 1c forced-picker pick.

**Prevention.** New mechanical sub-step for any future §4 Step 1c forced-picker write: when the director picks a specific `(a.NN)` / `P-NN` candidate in option (A) "Continue platform-wide work on main — name a specific task" (or any equivalent named-task option in (B)/(C)/(D)), the pointer-author MUST `grep -n "(a\.NN)\|P-NN" docs/ROADMAP.md` and read the matched status lines BEFORE writing the launch prompt around it. If status is `✅ SHIPPED` / `✅ DEPLOYED` / `✅ DONE` / `✅ COMPLETE`, the pointer-author surfaces the discrepancy to the director immediately ("the candidate you named is already marked ✅; did you mean to verify-and-close, or did you mean a different task?") rather than writing the launch prompt as ship-new. This is a Rule 24 extension applied at the pointer-write moment — captured as a deferred item this session (TaskCreate `DEFERRED:`) so the next process-doc edit formalizes it into HANDOFF_PROTOCOL §4 Step 1c text.

**Cross-references:**
- `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` 2026-05-18-c version (yesterday's misframed pointer; rewritten by today's end-of-session doc batch).
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` line 113 (the authoritative `**Status:** ✅ SHIPPED at code level 2026-05-14` line).
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` "Previously updated: May 14, 2026" header (full 2026-05-14 P-17 ship session narrative).
- `tests/playwright/authFetch-regression.spec.ts` (the existing P-17 test).
- `playwright.config.ts:5-9` (documents the P-17 suite + 2026-05-14 ship date).
- Commit `422c658` (the P-17 ship commit).
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 3 (code wins) + Rule 24 (pre-capture search; applicable to NEXT_SESSION.md pointer writes too) + §4 Step 1c (forced-picker for "no obvious next task").

### 2026-05-17 — Doc-vs-code drift on P-27 back-end status caught at start-of-session per Rule 3 (INFORMATIONAL — no production impact)

**Session:** `session_2026-05-17_w2-p28-p27-paired-build` (Claude Code, on `workflow-2-competition-scraping`).
**Tool/Phase affected:** W#2 Competition Scraping (PLOS-side) — design-picker phase.
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL (Low). No production impact; no time wasted on false-premise design; pattern lesson captured.

**What happened:** At start of the P-28 + P-27 paired build session, the `NEXT_SESSION.md` pre-build read list + the `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md` P-27 polish-backlog section both framed the back-end DELETE handlers for captured-text + captured-image as missing — claims like *"Back-end `text/route.ts` exposes GET + POST only — no DELETE handler. Need to build a new DELETE handler"* + *"Back-end `images/route.ts` exposes GET only — no DELETE handler. Need to build a new DELETE handler."* The original 5-question Rule 14f forced-picker set in `NEXT_SESSION.md` (Q1 soft-vs-hard delete / Q2 image storage cleanup / Q3 audit-trail / Q4 permission model / Q5 confirm-dialog placement) was structured around those framings — Q1-Q4 assumed greenfield back-end design choices were still open.

Verification at session start (Rule 24 sub-step d "verify against actual code" before any picker fires): `find` + `Read` against `src/app/api/projects/[projectId]/competition-scraping/text/[textId]/route.ts` + `images/[imageId]/route.ts` showed BOTH per-row DELETE handlers ALREADY EXIST — production-quality, idempotent on P2025, ownership-scoped via `competitorUrl: { projectWorkflowId }` relation filter, with the image DELETE additionally handling best-effort Supabase storage cleanup + janitor cron for orphans (per `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_STACK_DECISIONS.md §3`). The URL DELETE at `urls/[urlId]/route.ts:273` similarly already cascades via Prisma `onDelete: Cascade`. The handlers shipped during the original session-1 API-routes work — they're not new gaps.

**Consequence (avoided):** had the design pickers fired as originally planned, the director would have been asked to make back-end-architecture-shape decisions (soft-vs-hard delete schema design / cascade implementation strategy / per-row audit events) that were already settled by the existing production code. Rule 3 (code wins over doc) protected the picker from operating on a false premise.

**Root cause:** the P-27 polish-backlog entry was captured 2026-05-14 in the deploy-session-#13 batch BEFORE Rule 24 pre-capture search had read against the relevant code paths. The capture's "What's shipped today (verified 2026-05-14 via code-read)" sub-section was actually wrong against code reality even at the time of capture — possibly because the 2026-05-14 verification searched for "DELETE handler in `urls/[urlId]/text/route.ts`" (the collection route, which is GET + POST only) and missed the sibling per-row routes at `text/[textId]/route.ts` + `images/[imageId]/route.ts` that actually carry DELETE. The collection route's path is one folder shallower than the per-row route, and the 2026-05-14 verification likely conflated the two.

**How caught:** Rule 24 sub-step d + Rule 3 fired automatically at start-of-session per Claude's standing operational behavior. The verification step "verify the back-end DELETE handlers exist by reading the route files" surfaced the drift immediately, BEFORE drafting the forced-pickers + BEFORE the director sent "Go ahead."

**Correction:** at session start, Claude surfaced the full diff between doc claims + code reality to the director in a structured drift-check section ("🚨 Load-bearing finding"), reframed the session scope from "back-end + UI build" → "UI-only build + new cascade-counts endpoint," collapsed the originally-planned Q1-Q5 picker set to two genuinely-open questions (Q5 confirm-dialog component placement + Q6 cascade-count mechanism for P-28 disclosure), and fired only those two via AskUserQuestion. Director picked recommended options on both; build proceeded against the correct premise. Doc fix is captured in this batch — both `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md`'s P-27 entry + `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN.md` §B 2026-05-17 entry now reflect code reality.

**Prevention:** for any future polish-backlog entry that frames a route under an existing workflow as "needs NEW back-end DELETE/PATCH handler," the capturing session MUST run `find` or `ls` against the actual route directory + Read the matched `route.ts` BEFORE writing the "What's shipped today" sub-section. The Rule 24 sub-step d ("verify against actual code") already covers this in principle; this entry captures a concrete failure mode where the verify-against-code step looked at the wrong route file (collection vs. per-row). When a workflow has BOTH a collection route (`<area>/route.ts`) AND a per-row route (`<area>/[id]/route.ts`), the polish capture must check BOTH — PATCH + DELETE typically live on the per-row file, not the collection file. The slip would also have been caught by a separate "back-end exhaustively listed" sub-step at capture time (running `grep -l "DELETE" -r .../<area>/`) — adding that grep to the polish-capture procedure is the mechanical fix.

**Cross-references:**
- `src/app/api/projects/[projectId]/competition-scraping/text/[textId]/route.ts:171` (the existing text DELETE handler).
- `src/app/api/projects/[projectId]/competition-scraping/images/[imageId]/route.ts:209` (the existing image DELETE handler).
- `src/app/api/projects/[projectId]/competition-scraping/urls/[urlId]/route.ts:273` (the existing URL DELETE handler with cascade via Prisma schema).
- `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md` 2026-05-14 P-27 entry (the stale framing — now flipped to ✅ SHIPPED-AT-CODE-LEVEL with correct framing 2026-05-17).
- `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN.md` §B 2026-05-17 entry (full session narrative).
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 3 (code wins over doc — applied this session) + Rule 24 sub-step d (verify against actual code — fired at start-of-session) + Rule 14f (forced-picker shape used for Q5 + Q6).

### 2026-05-16 — CWD-leak Bash slip recurred TWICE in one session — pre-deploy scoreboard + post-merge scoreboard both bit by the same pattern (INFORMATIONAL — 4th-or-5th recurrence of the 2026-05-12-c slip class; pattern STRENGTHENING)

**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL.

**What happened.** Twice this session (W#2 → main deploy session #16), running parallel Bash calls where one command included `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile` (or similar `cd`-into-subdir command), the working directory leaked to subsequent commands in the same parallel batch. Specifically:

- **Instance 1 (pre-deploy scoreboard on workflow-2):** Bash call #2 ran `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile`. Bash call #3 in the same parallel batch ran `find src/lib -name "*.test.ts" -print0 | xargs -0 node --test --experimental-strip-types` expecting to find the 18 src/lib test files (target 509/509 tests). The `find` ran from the extension directory instead of repo root, found a different set of test files, and printed **334 tests** (the extension's count) instead of the expected 509. Caught immediately on inspecting the output.
- **Instance 2 (post-merge scoreboard on main):** identical pattern — Bash call ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile` (this one used absolute path, but the cwd-state still propagated to subsequent commands in the same batch); the parallel `find src/lib` call inherited the extension cwd and again returned 334.

**Recovery (both instances):** explicit `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && find src/lib ...` prefix on the failing command. Re-ran. Got the correct 509/509.

**Net cost.** ~30 seconds total (one re-run per instance; two instances total). Zero production impact — the scoreboard was being run for verification BEFORE any push, so the false-negative would have been caught before any production change.

**Recurrence history.** This is the 4th-or-5th time this slip class has fired:
- `2026-05-12-c` — original capture (`cd <subdir>` Bash-tool invocation effectively persisted)
- `2026-05-13` — recurred
- `2026-05-14` (W#2 → main deploy session #12) — recurred per CHAT_REGISTRY: *"two small operational slips recurred today — both self-caught"*; deliberate decision NOT to add a 3rd identical entry to avoid noise
- `2026-05-15-d` — recurred (separate entry written, see line 258)
- `2026-05-16` (this session) — TWICE in one session

**Why this entry exists despite prior "no need to repeat" decisions.** Two instances in ONE session is a meaningful signal. The prior 2026-05-14 decision was made when this had recurred 1× per session. Pattern fired 2× this session means the existing operational discipline isn't being applied consistently mid-flight. Worth strengthening with a more mechanical rule.

**Pattern STRENGTHENING — operational rule going forward.**

When running parallel Bash calls in a single message:
- **If ANY command in the batch uses `cd <subdir>`:** EVERY command in the batch MUST start with `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && ...` (or another explicit absolute cwd) — even commands that don't apparently need it. The Bash tool's shared cwd-state means a "downstream" command can be silently affected by an "upstream" command's cd, even when they're conceptually independent.
- **Preference: avoid `cd` entirely when possible.** Use absolute paths like `find /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/src/lib -name "*.test.ts"` or run npm commands via `npm --prefix /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/extensions/competition-scraping run compile`. This eliminates the entire class of slip.
- **Verification tell:** if a test run reports a count you don't expect (especially a count that matches a DIFFERENT subdirectory's tests — e.g., 334 instead of 509), suspect cwd-leak FIRST before suspecting the test code changed.

**Cross-references:**
- `CORRECTIONS_LOG.md` 2026-05-12-c entry (original) — line ~623
- `CORRECTIONS_LOG.md` 2026-05-15-d entry — line ~258
- `CHAT_REGISTRY.md` 2026-05-14 deploy #12 entry — "no new entry" decision context

### 2026-05-15-h — Rule 14f forced-picker for P-31 DI seam shape shown before verifying the test-runner constraint that `next/server` could not be imported under `node --test --experimental-strip-types` (INFORMATIONAL — surfaced honestly mid-flight; ran a second picker; net cost ~3 min)

**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL — the slip surfaced as a structural constraint discovery, not a wrong-decision shipped to code. The first picker ran, director picked Option A, I wrote the factory, the first probe immediately revealed the constraint, I surfaced honestly and ran a second picker. No false-premise decision was acted on; net cost ~3 minutes of director time + one extra picker round.

**The slip:**

For the W#2 P-31 route-handler DI refactor, the launch prompt instructed me to surface a Rule 14f forced-picker comparing three candidate DI seam shapes:
- (A) Per-route closure factory `makeHandler(deps): (req, ctx) => Promise<NextResponse>` — production wires real deps at module load; tests construct `new NextRequest(...)` + fakes and exercise the returned handler.
- (B) Module-level pure function `createCompetitorUrl({...deps}): Promise<{ status, body }>` — fully testable; no Next.js types in test surface.
- (C) Three-layer adapter pattern.

I drafted the picker, marked Option B as `(recommended)` per `feedback_recommendation_style.md`, and showed it to the director. **Director picked Option A.**

I started implementing the factory + the first test file. Wrote `urls.ts` factory + `urls.test.ts` with `import { NextRequest } from 'next/server'`. Tried to run `node --test --experimental-strip-types src/lib/competition-scraping/handlers/urls.test.ts` — failed:

```
Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find package 'next/server' imported from .../urls.test.ts
```

Probed Next's `package.json`:

```js
exports = { ... }  // server keys: []
```

`next/server` is NOT in Next's `package.json` `exports` field — only top-level `server.js` + `server.d.ts` files exist at `node_modules/next/`, but no subpath export points at them. Node ESM resolution cannot reach the subpath. The factory file imports + the test file imports both fail under the existing `node --test --experimental-strip-types` infrastructure.

This is a structural constraint of the existing test runner. Option A's premise (tests construct NextRequest objects) was therefore not directly viable with the test runner the launch prompt's verification scoreboard relied on (`find src/lib -name "*.test.ts" | xargs node --test --experimental-strip-types`).

**What I did (Rule 10 acknowledge openly):**

Surfaced honestly to the director: "I missed verifying the test-runner constraint before showing the picker. Three honest paths forward: (A') adapt Option A to a RequestLike interface; (B) switch to Option B my original recommendation; (C) add vitest+next plugin (significant infra investment)." Ran a second Rule 14f forced-picker. Director picked **Option A' — RequestLike contract in the factory; Next types only at the route boundary**. Net cost ~3 min.

**Root cause:**

I treated the test-runner mechanism as a "known fact" rather than as a constraint to verify before showing the picker. The check was a 30-second probe (`cat > probe.test.ts <<EOF; node --test EOF`), and the cost of skipping it was ~3 minutes director time + one extra picker round. The check should have been part of "drafting the Rule 14f picker", not "after the director chose".

**Pattern lesson:**

When a Rule 14f forced-picker involves a test-runner / build-tool / module-loader mechanism (node:test / vitest / playwright / esbuild / etc.), the picker draft must include a 30-second probe of the smallest viable case BEFORE surfacing the picker. If the probe surfaces a constraint, the picker's option set + recommendation marker may change; the picker should reflect the constraint as part of each option's description.

**Concrete operational test going forward:**

Before surfacing a Rule 14f picker whose options involve a test-runner / build-tool / module-loader mechanism:
1. Pick the option set's most "structurally aggressive" shape (the one with strongest assumptions about the mechanism — typically the "no abstraction" or "purest" option).
2. Write a 5-line probe that exercises the assumption (e.g., for Option A's "tests construct NextRequest", a probe is `import { NextRequest } from 'next/server'; new NextRequest('http://x/')`).
3. Run the probe.
4. If it succeeds: surface the picker as drafted.
5. If it fails: re-draft the picker with the constraint surfaced + adjusted option set.

The probe cost is ~30 seconds; the cost of skipping it is ~3 minutes director time PLUS the operational confusion of "we just picked something that's now broken."

**Relationship to existing rules:**

- Rule 14a (Read-It-Back test) — applies to questions, not pickers' options.
- Rule 14b/14f (multi-option question structure) — governs HOW the picker is shaped; not WHEN to verify its premises.
- Rule 24 (pre-capture search) — applies to ROADMAP captures; the spirit (verify before write) generalizes to pickers too.
- This entry adds the operational scaffolding for "verify the test-runner / build-tool / module-loader mechanism's premise" specifically at the moment of drafting a Rule 14f picker that involves one.

**Cross-references:**

- COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN.md §B 2026-05-15-h entry (the session that surfaced the slip)
- COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md new "## P-31 SHIPPED at code level" section (the design pick + implementation outcome)
- `src/lib/competition-scraping/handlers/{shared,urls,url-text,images-finalize,images-fetch-by-url}.ts` (the factory files; all imports avoid `next/server`)
- 4 route.ts shim files (the production wrapping that imports `next/server` only at the route boundary)

**Director feedback:** none captured this session — the slip was caught + recovered within ~3 min of surfacing; director moved on to the second picker without commentary.

### 2026-05-15-d — Extension tsc test-file fixture slip carried from Slice #1's verification scoreboard gap (INFORMATIONAL — caught + fixed inline this session)

**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL — caught immediately when Fix #5 (P-36) needed `npm run compile` for the extension; runtime impact zero (wxt build still produced a clean Chrome MV3 bundle; the type error was confined to a test file the production build excludes).

**What happened.** During deploy session #14's Fix #5 verification, ran `npm run compile` (alias for `tsc --noEmit`) inside `extensions/competition-scraping/`. tsc emitted:

```
src/lib/captured-text-validation.test.ts(16,7): error TS2741:
Property 'source' is missing in type '{ id: string; projectWorkflowId: string;
platform: "amazon"; url: string; ... }' but required in type 'CompetitorUrl'.
```

The `CompetitorUrl` test fixture `ROW_A` was missing the `source` field added by Slice #1 (2026-05-15-b) when the shared-types `CompetitorUrl` interface gained `source: Source`. The fixture was never updated.

**Root cause.** Slice #1's session verification scoreboard ran `npx tsc --noEmit` from the **repo root**, which uses the root `tsconfig.json` and does not include the extension's TypeScript sources. The extension has its own `tsconfig.json` and its own compile script (`cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile`) that does include those files. The Slice #1 session only ran the root tsc, so the test-fixture-vs-interface drift wasn't caught.

**Fix applied this session (in the doc-batch commit).** Added `source: 'extension'` to `ROW_A`. All test files inheriting from `ROW_A` via spread (`{ ...ROW_A, ... }`) now compile clean. Re-ran `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile` — clean. Re-ran the same after Fix #5's `wxt build` — clean. Repo-root `npx tsc --noEmit` was clean throughout (it never had the error).

**Lesson — verification scoreboard scope.** Slice sessions that touch shared-types must run BOTH the root tsc AND `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile` in their verification scoreboard, because the extension's tsconfig has additional file coverage. Update applied to operational practice; future P-29-Slice / shared-types-touching slice sessions should include extension tsc in the scoreboard. Not yet codified in a HANDOFF_PROTOCOL rule — adding to operational memory only at this time per the principle of "rule-promotion when recurrence is observed twice." First observation here.

**Cross-references:** `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/captured-text-validation.test.ts` (the fixture file fixed); `src/lib/shared-types/competition-scraping.ts` (the `CompetitorUrl` interface where `source` was added by Slice #1); commit `070820a` (Slice #1 ship that introduced the gap); Slice #1's end-of-session header in CHAT_REGISTRY 2026-05-15-b (gap window).

### 2026-05-15-d — CWD-drift Bash slip recurrence — early `cd docs/` from a Read failure cascade + later `cd extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile` persisted across subsequent Bash calls until reset to repo root with absolute paths (INFORMATIONAL — recurrence of 2026-05-12-c slip class; caught + recovered same session)

**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL — caught + recovered before any incorrect state was committed; no production impact.

**What happened.** Mid-session, ran the pre-deploy verification scoreboard. The TypeScript and node:test commands succeeded but produced suspicious results (e.g., `Could not find 'src/lib/shared-types/competition-scraping.test.ts'` from the node:test run). Diagnosis: an earlier Bash call had used `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/docs && ...` and the CWD persisted across subsequent Bash invocations. Reset to repo root via `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && pwd && ...` and re-ran with explicit absolute paths.

Later in the same session, during Fix #5's extension build work, ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/extensions/competition-scraping && npm run compile`. The CWD persisted again — subsequent `git status` calls from a subdirectory still worked correctly (git operates on the working tree from any subdir) but `npx tsc --noEmit` and `npm run build` would have run from the wrong directory if I'd called them without absolute paths. Recovered by `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && git status` to reset CWD before the doc-batch work.

**Root cause.** Per Bash tool docs: "The working directory persists between commands, but shell state does not." But operationally I've forgotten this twice in the same session, the same way: assuming each Bash call starts in repo root when in fact the previous `cd <subdir>` persists. Same slip-class as `session_2026-05-12-c` CORRECTIONS_LOG entry: "`cd <subdir>` Bash-tool invocation effectively persisted as the session's cwd; subsequent 'root build' commands silently ran from extension dir."

**Recurrence flag.** This is the SECOND observation of the slip class (2026-05-12-c + 2026-05-15-d). Per rule-promotion principle, two recurrences justifies codification into operational practice — captured in CLAUDE.md guidance "Try to maintain your current working directory throughout the session by using absolute paths and avoiding usage of `cd`." Going forward: prefer `subdir/path/to/file` from repo root over `cd subdir && file`; when `cd` is unavoidable (e.g., `npm run compile` that uses the workspace's own `package.json`), explicitly `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && ...` to reset BEFORE the next conceptual unit of work.

**Cross-references:** CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-05-12-c entry "`cd <subdir>` Bash-tool invocation effectively persisted" (the canonical prior observation); CORRECTIONS_LOG 2026-05-14 entry "Working-directory drift across Bash calls" (a third related observation but for a different mechanism — there it was the user-prompt-submit-hook running from a different cwd).

### 2026-05-15 — Q2 code-vs-doc drift caught at session start: yesterday's polish-backlog P-29 entry framed "Other" platform handling as requiring a schema add, but current schema is already a String column with `independent-website` as a supported value end-to-end (INFORMATIONAL — caught BEFORE the Q2 forced-picker fired; no false-premise decision was made; DATA_CATALOG.md §6.1.1 underscores-vs-hyphens drift also captured + fixed inline this batch)

**Session:** session_2026-05-15_w2-p29-design-session (Claude Code; on `workflow-2-competition-scraping`)
**Tool/Phase affected:** W#2 Competition Scraping — polish-item capture discipline (Rule 24 pre-capture search sub-step d).
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL — drift caught BEFORE any decision was made on the incorrect premise; no rework cost.
**Type:** PROCESS — Rule 24 sub-step (d) "verify against actual code" was implicit but partially applied during yesterday's P-29 capture.

**What happened (the Q2 framing drift).** Yesterday's W#2 → main deploy session #13 captured three new polish items (P-27 / P-28 / P-29) end-of-session per director's expand-roadmap ask. The P-29 entry in `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md` line 1832 stated: *"Current platform enum is `walmart | ebay | etsy | amazon` (confirmed via grep on schema + `PlatformId` type). Adding 'other' requires a schema add (enum extension or shift to string-typed column)."* This claim shaped the launch prompt's Q2 framing for today's design session: *"'Other' platform option — schema add: new enum value `other` on `PlatformId` (additive enum, low risk) vs. shift to string-typed platform field..."*. Today's start-of-session Rule 24 verification check found that the claim was wrong against current code.

**Actual code state (verified today via Rule-3 verify-against-actual-code):**
- `prisma/schema.prisma:257` — `platform String` (a String column, NOT a Prisma enum). Schema comment lists 7 supported values.
- `src/lib/shared-types/competition-scraping.ts:20-29` — `PLATFORMS` const array of 7 strings: `amazon | ebay | etsy | walmart | google-shopping | google-ads | independent-website`.
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/platforms.ts:13-19` — extension popup PlatformPicker exposes all 7 with "Independent Website" as the user-visible label for the 7th option.

So the director's "Other" use case from DESIGN doc line 489 ("manually add a url into the competition table (for example, independent websites)") is **already representable in today's schema** — no schema migration needed for "Other" handling. Q2 collapsed from "schema add yes/no?" to a Rule 15 autonomous label-copy call ("use existing `independent-website` value; label it 'Independent Website' in the manual-add modal to match extension").

**Root cause.** Yesterday's pre-capture search (Rule 24) found the higher-level lineage on DESIGN doc line 489 (the director's "independent websites" intent), confirmed P-29 was a partial-implementation of original W#2 design-doc intent, and noted back-end POSTs already exist for the manual-add path. But the search **did not include a grep on `prisma/schema.prisma` for the actual `platform` column type or on `src/lib/shared-types/competition-scraping.ts` for the `PLATFORMS` const**. Without that verify-against-actual-code step, the claim "platform enum is 4 values, schema add required" was carried forward from working memory (likely a stale framing from an earlier W#2 design moment) rather than confirmed against today's code state.

**Secondary drift caught in this same batch (fixed inline):** `DATA_CATALOG.md` §6.1.1 line 318 lists the platform enum values using underscores (`google_shopping`, `independent_website`) while the code uses hyphens (`google-shopping`, `independent-website`). Minor cosmetic doc drift; fixed inline this end-of-session doc batch via direct edit (Rule 3 — code wins). Doesn't affect the Q2 design decision since the value count + names are correct, just the punctuation.

**How caught.** Claude self-caught during today's start-of-session Rule 24 pre-capture-search verification. The drift was surfaced in the start-of-session drift check BEFORE any Q2 forced-picker fired. Director was given the corrected premise; Q2 collapsed accordingly; no false-premise decision was made.

**Correction.** Q2 forced-picker was reshaped on the corrected premise (Stage 0 of the design session). No schema change in P-29 for "Other" handling. Modal labels existing `independent-website` value as "Independent Website" matching extension. DATA_CATALOG.md §6.1.1 underscore→hyphen drift fixed inline this batch.

**Prevention.** Rule 24 already includes sub-step (d): *"Verify against actual code when the concern relates to specific behavior. Use `Read` against the relevant source files... and not just the doc claims."* This sub-step exists in the rule but was applied PARTIALLY in yesterday's capture — the lineage check (against DESIGN doc) was thorough, but the schema-state check was carried from working memory. **Operational reinforcement for future polish-item captures:** when a new polish item makes a specific claim about current schema/code state (column type, enum values, route shape, etc.), Rule 24 sub-step (d) MUST fire — grep the actual code, then cite the file + line in the polish-item entry so future sessions can verify the claim independently. This discipline is captured here as Claude-side operational practice; will reinforce in future polish-item captures by including explicit "schema state at capture time: [grep result, file:line]" lines in the polish-item entries that make schema claims.

**Cross-references:**
- `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG.md` line 1832 (the incorrect claim — left in place as the historical record per design-doc append-only discipline; today's design-pass §B entry supersedes it)
- `prisma/schema.prisma:257` (actual schema state — String column, 7 supported values)
- `src/lib/shared-types/competition-scraping.ts:20-29` (actual `PLATFORMS` const)
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/platforms.ts:13-19` (actual extension popup picker)
- `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 24 sub-step (d) — verify against actual code (the existing rule that, if fully applied, prevents this drift class)
- `COMPETITION_SCRAPING_DESIGN.md` §B 2026-05-15 entry (Q2-reframing captured + cross-referenced)
- `DATA_CATALOG.md` §6.1.1 line 318 (underscore→hyphen drift fixed inline this batch)

### 2026-05-14 — `.claude/settings.json` hook paths were relative; failed open (exit 127) on every Bash call from a subdir; surfaced today via 53 visible UI noise warnings in this session, with a latent reliability gap that effectively disabled HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 28 Layer 4 whenever Claude committed from a subdir (NEAR-MISS — fixed same session)

**Session:** session_2026-05-14_w2-main-deploy-session-13-p23-amazon-context-menu-DEPLOYED-FULL-VERIFY (Claude Code; landed on `main`)
**Tool/Phase affected:** Claude Code hook system — both PreToolUse Bash hook (`check-next-session-doc.sh`, the NEXT_SESSION.md guard) and SessionStart hook (`inject-next-session-pointer.sh`, the resume-flow pointer-injector).
**Severity:** NEAR-MISS. No data loss today — the actual end-of-session `git commit` happened from repo-root cwd so the guard hook fired correctly. But the bug was a real reliability gap: any "End-of-session" commit issued from a subdir would have slipped past Layer 4 of Rule 28's multi-layered defense, silently failing the load-bearing guarantee that the NEXT_SESSION.md guard was created to enforce.
**Type:** PROCESS + CONFIGURATION — hook wiring shape worth capturing as a general lesson.

**What happened.** Director noticed many "PreToolUse:Bash hook errors" in their UI mid-session and asked Claude what was firing. Claude couldn't see them directly (the harness only surfaces hook stderr to Claude when the hook BLOCKS a tool call via exit-2 — non-blocking exit-127 errors go to the user UI but not to Claude). Claude inspected the session transcript JSONL file and found 53 `hook_non_blocking_error` attachments, all identical:

```
stderr: Failed with non-blocking status code: /bin/sh: 1: .claude/hooks/check-next-session-doc.sh: not found
exitCode: 127
```

Root cause: `.claude/settings.json` wired both hooks with **relative paths** (`".claude/hooks/check-next-session-doc.sh"` + `".claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh"`). The harness runs each hook command from the cwd of the Bash call. When Claude `cd`-ed into subdirs (`extensions/competition-scraping` for ext build/test/tsc; `.output/chrome-mv3` for zip packaging; etc.), the relative path didn't resolve and `/bin/sh` returned exit-127 "not found." The harness classified this as a non-blocking error, so:

1. **Cosmetic noise:** ~53 visible warnings in the director's UI in this single session. Future W#2 sessions touching the extension subdir would generate similar volumes.
2. **Latent reliability gap:** the NEXT_SESSION.md guard at Layer 4 of Rule 28's multi-layered defense effectively was disabled whenever Claude committed from a subdir. Today the actual end-of-session commit happened from repo root so the guard fired correctly — but this was a coincidence, not a guarantee.

**Lesson — generalizable.** Hooks wired in `.claude/settings.json` MUST use absolute paths or the `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` substitution token for executable paths. Relative paths in hook commands are cwd-dependent and will fail open (exit 127) on any Bash call whose cwd differs from repo root — which is most Bash calls in a multi-workflow PLOS session (extension build, test, zip, etc.).

**Cross-cuts Rule 28's test discipline.** HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 28 line 626 reads: *"every time a new session ships changes to `.claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh`, `.claude/settings.json` SessionStart entry, `./resume`, or `docs/CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` sentinel section, the session-AFTER-ship is responsible for verifying the full flow end-to-end."* The flow-end-to-end test focuses on the SessionStart hook firing during a `./resume` launch — which works correctly even when run from repo root. The Bash-from-subdir failure mode wasn't part of the documented verification surface. **Rule 28 should be extended:** the post-ship verification must ALSO run a Bash command from a subdir (e.g., `cd extensions/competition-scraping && pwd`) and confirm zero exit-127 hook errors land in the transcript.

**Fix shipped same session:** one-line edit in `.claude/settings.json` — both hook command entries changed from `".claude/hooks/<name>.sh"` to `"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/hooks/<name>.sh"`. Smoke-tested by running two Bash commands from subdirs (`extensions/competition-scraping` and `.output/chrome-mv3`); transcript inspection confirmed zero new `hook_non_blocking_error` attachments after the fix. Committed + pushed to `main` as a small follow-up after the W#2 → main deploy session #13 doc batch.

**Cross-references:**
- `.claude/settings.json` (the fix site).
- `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 28 line 626 (the test-discipline rule that needs extension to cover this failure mode).
- `.claude/hooks/check-next-session-doc.sh` + `.claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh` (the hook scripts — unchanged; only the wiring was buggy).
- The session's transcript JSONL at `/home/codespace/.claude/projects/-workspaces-brand-operations-hub/5be2ffa9-5b2e-4517-9005-18c76046b7de.jsonl` (where the 53 historical errors live, all timestamped before the fix at ~21:47 UTC 2026-05-14).

### 2026-05-14 — Playwright capture-phase listener-attach race in P-23 spec; placed listener after async init in orchestrator; caught via first test run, fixed via source re-architecture (INFORMATIONAL — content-script test-shape lesson worth recording)

**Session:** session_2026-05-14_w2-polish-session-18-p23-amazon-right-click-context-menu-SHIP (Claude Code, on `workflow-2-competition-scraping`)
**Tool/Phase affected:** Extension-context Playwright test workflow; specifically the timing relationship between `data-plos-cs-active=1` (the body marker Playwright keys off) and orchestrator-attached event listeners.
**Severity:** INFORMATIONAL (caught + fixed before commit; no production impact; ~10 minutes of investigation + a small source re-architecture).
**Type:** PROCESS — content-script test-shape gotcha worth capturing for future capture-phase listener tests.

**What happened.** P-23 fix needed a `document.addEventListener('contextmenu', ..., { capture: true })` listener in the orchestrator that walks up from the right-click target to find an underlying `<img>` and caches its src in `lastRightClickImageSrc`. My first draft placed the listener attach AFTER several `await` calls in `runOrchestrator` (after the `await Promise.all([getSelectedProjectId, getSelectedPlatform])` + `await listCompetitorUrls` + `await listProjects` + `await startLiveHighlighting`) — alongside the other orchestrator init affordances. The Playwright spec then ran:

1. `page.waitForFunction(() => document.body.getAttribute('data-plos-cs-active') === '1')` — this signal is set at the TOP of `runOrchestrator` (line 79), BEFORE any awaits.
2. `page.evaluate(() => dispatchEvent('contextmenu' on overlay-shield))` — fires synchronously after step 1 resolves.
3. `serviceWorker.evaluate(() => chrome.tabs.sendMessage('open-image-capture-form', { srcUrl: '' }))` — with retry loop until the orchestrator's `chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener` fires (which happens at the END of `runOrchestrator` init).

The bug: between step 1 (`data-plos-cs-active=1` synchronously set) and the contextmenu listener attach (after several async awaits), microtask yields occur. Playwright's step 2 dispatch can fire DURING those microtask yields — before the listener is attached. So the listener never sees the contextmenu event, the cache stays null, and step 3's empty-srcUrl message triggers the silent-bail path instead of the fallback path. Test fails with "form did not render" even though the production code paths are all correct.

**Why it surfaced cleanly.** The negative test (contextmenu on plain text → silent bail) PASSED because there's no image-to-find anyway — bail-silently was the expected outcome. The positive test failed because the cache was empty when the message arrived. The asymmetry between the two test outcomes localized the bug to "the cache isn't populating" → which pointed at the listener-attach timing.

**Fix.** Hoist the contextmenu listener attach to the TOP of `runOrchestrator` — immediately after the body-marker `data-plos-cs-active=1` is set, BEFORE any awaits. Both are synchronous on the same JavaScript tick, so by the time Playwright observes `data-plos-cs-active=1`, the listener is attached. Also added a `detachContextMenuListener()` helper called from each of the 3 early-return paths (`!projectId/!platform`, `!platformModule`, `wrong hostname`) so the listener doesn't leak on unsupported platforms.

**Lessons captured:**

- **Content-script tests that exercise capture-phase event listeners must attach the listener BEFORE the `data-plos-cs-active=1` signal Playwright keys off.** The body marker is the "orchestrator started" signal; any listener attached AFTER it has a race window where Playwright can fire events before the listener is wired.
- **OR introduce a separate "listeners ready" signal** (e.g., a second body attribute `data-plos-cs-listeners-ready=1`) and have the Playwright spec wait for it instead. The former (attach before the marker) is simpler when the listener doesn't depend on async-resolved state; the latter is needed when listener wiring requires the orchestrator's full init.
- **The asymmetric positive-vs-negative test outcome is a useful diagnostic.** A timing race typically shows up as one direction passing and the other failing in a way that doesn't match the production behavior. When the test failure pattern looks like "the test setup is wrong, not the production code is wrong," check signal-vs-listener-attach ordering first.
- **Capture-phase listener detach in early returns** — when the listener attaches up front but the orchestrator's early-return paths fire (unsupported platform / no setup / wrong hostname), the listener must be detached explicitly because the caller's `ctx.onInvalidated(cleanup)` won't fire for the no-op `() => undefined` cleanups returned in those paths. The listener-leak is benign (just updates a never-read variable) but cleanup-on-no-op is the cleaner shape.

**Cross-references:**
- `extensions/competition-scraping/src/lib/content-script/orchestrator.ts` — the file where the fix landed (contextmenu listener now at top of `runOrchestrator`, with `detachContextMenuListener` helper called from each early-return path + the main cleanup function).
- `tests/playwright/extension/p23-amazon-overlay-image.spec.ts` — the spec that surfaced the race.
- Earlier Playwright extension-context test lessons (2026-05-12-e on `.output/` staleness; 2026-05-14 on `context.route` LIFO matching) — same parent theme: extension-context Playwright has more gotchas than vanilla Playwright; explicit reasoning-before-action / careful signal-ordering / explicit-listener-detach saves debug cycles.
- HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md Rule 16 (zoom-in/zoom-out) — applied here: the production-code "where should this listener live in the orchestrator's init flow?" question has the same answer regardless of test concerns (top of function, before awaits, for the lowest race risk on the production-code path too — real users right-clicking during page load benefit from earlier listener attachment).

### 2026-05-14 — Resume-flow Rule 28 multi-layered defense verified working end-to-end on first real run (INFORMATIONAL — closes the test-discipline obligation from yesterday's fix session)

**What happened.** Yesterday's same-session follow-up (`session_2026-05-14_w2-main-deploy-session-11-region-screenshot-DEPLOYED-FULL-VERIFY` + Rule 28 ship) coded a four-layer defense for `./resume`:

- Layer 1 — `SessionStart` hook (`.claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh`) injects `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` contents as a system reminder before the user's first prompt.
- Layer 2 — `CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` sentinel-string match procedural fallback.
- Layer 3 — Documented 3-step manual ESCAPE HATCH.
- Layer 4 — `.claude/hooks/check-next-session-doc.sh` PreToolUse guard on end-of-session commits.

Rule 28's "Test discipline" clause required the session AFTER the ship to verify the full flow end-to-end. Today is that session. **Outcome: Layer 1 fired correctly.** The session's first system reminder contained the `🟢 RESUME-FLOW POINTER — docs/NEXT_SESSION.md content follows ...` marker emitted by the hook. Claude read the injected pointer content as the launch prompt, ran the standard start-of-session sequence (branch verification → HANDOFF_PROTOCOL read → MULTI_WORKFLOW_PROTOCOL read → P-20 design pass), and the director's wake-up keystroke (`Tool loaded.` — the short post-tool-load acknowledgement) was sufficient to begin work. No manual paste of the launch prompt was needed.

**Why this is worth recording.** Yesterday's CORRECTIONS_LOG entry "Resume-flow design flaw" captured Lesson #1: *"Don't ship a CLI-integration design without testing the full flow on a real session start."* The 2026-05-13-c original `./resume` ship deferred its end-to-end verification with the rationale "first real run IS the verification" — and the first real run failed in front of the director. Yesterday's Rule 28 fix carried the same architectural deferral, so today's verification was the load-bearing test of whether the multi-layered redesign actually solves what the original single-mechanism design did not. **Today's pass confirms the redesign is sound.** Both Layer 1 (mechanical, primary) and Layer 4 (mechanical, end-of-session enforcement) require no Claude-side discipline; Layers 2 and 3 are still untested in real failure modes but available as documented fallbacks.

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — no production impact; entirely a process verification observation.

**Cross-references.** Yesterday's "Resume-flow design flaw" entry (the failure that prompted Rule 28); `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 28 (the four-layer codification); `CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` "Resume-flow handling" section; `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` step 5 of today's "Test the fix end-to-end" instructions (which this entry closes); `.claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh` (Layer 1 — the hook that did the work today).

### 2026-05-14 — Working-directory drift across Bash calls + pipe-buffered `tail -N` in background commands (INFORMATIONAL — two small operational slips, both self-caught and recovered)

**What happened.** Two small operational slips during the P-20 ship's test cycle today; neither affected correctness, but both wasted a few minutes:

**(1) Working-directory drift.** Earlier in the session I ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && cat playwright.config.ts ...` to inspect the Playwright config at repo root. That `cd` persisted across subsequent Bash calls (per Bash tool docs: "The working directory persists between commands"). My next command was `npm run build` without re-entering the extension directory — it ran at repo root and built the Next.js app instead of the WXT extension. Caught when the output showed Next.js routes + middleware instead of WXT bundle sizes. Fixed by re-running with `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub/extensions/competition-scraping && npm run build`.

**(2) Pipe-buffered `tail -N` in `run_in_background: true`.** I had set up the extension build as a background command piped through `tail -25`. The pipe buffers stdout until the pipeline closes, so the background command's output file stayed at 0 bytes for the entire build run — couldn't observe progress. Combined with slip (1), I assumed the build was hanging and killed it, only to realize after the fact that it had actually completed (exit 0) but the output had been buffered. Re-ran synchronously with `timeout 90 npx wxt build > /tmp/wxt-build.log 2>&1` (no pipe) — completed in 1.3 seconds with full output visible immediately.

**Lessons:**

- **Prefer absolute paths over `cd`** per the Bash tool's own guidance: "Try to maintain your current working directory throughout the session by using absolute paths and avoiding usage of `cd`." For one-off reads of files at non-default paths, use the absolute path in `cat` / `Read` / `grep` rather than `cd`-ing there. When a directory change is genuinely required for a subsequent command (e.g., npm scripts that need to run from a specific package dir), bundle the `cd` AND the next command in the SAME Bash call with `&&`, so the directory drift doesn't persist into unrelated later commands.

- **For `run_in_background: true`, write output to a file, not through a pipe.** The harness's per-task output file (`/tmp/claude-1000/.../tasks/<id>.output`) is intended to be readable as the command runs, but only if stdout is unbuffered. Piping through `tail -N` or any other filter buffers until the pipeline closes — making the per-task output file useless for progress observation. Use `> file 2>&1` redirect to write directly, then `Read` or `tail` the file at observation time. Or use `Bash` synchronously when the command is bounded (a few seconds to a few minutes).

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — both slips were self-caught and recovered within a few minutes; no production impact; no incorrect work shipped.

**Cross-references.** Bash tool documentation (the "absolute paths" guidance + the "run_in_background" notes); `feedback_background_process_patience.md` operational memory (the related-but-distinct lesson about not asking the user "continue" when a background process can complete on its own).

---

### 2026-05-14 — Resume-flow design flaw: original `./resume` script's `exec claude "$SENTINEL"` doesn't auto-submit in interactive mode (MEDIUM severity — fixed same session with multi-layered defense per HANDOFF_PROTOCOL Rule 28)

**What happened.** Director ran `cd /workspaces/brand-operations-hub && ./resume` at the start of today's session (per `feedback_session_management.md` and the docs that promised a one-command session-handoff). The script ran successfully (switched branch, pulled latest, printed pointer contents to terminal, launched `claude`). But when Claude Code launched in interactive mode, it just sat waiting for input — the sentinel string passed via `exec claude "$SENTINEL"` was NOT auto-submitted as a first message. Director had to manually copy the launch prompt from the terminal scrollback and paste it as their first Claude Code message, defeating the "one-command" UX promise.

**Why it happened.** Claude Code's CLI documentation lists `[prompt]` as a positional argument and the original `./resume` design assumed it would auto-submit in interactive mode. In fact, the positional `[prompt]` **only auto-submits in non-interactive print mode** (`claude -p "..."`). In interactive mode (which `./resume` wants), the positional pre-fills the input box silently or is ignored entirely — director still has to manually press Enter or paste. This is documented behavior, but the original `./resume` design (shipped 2026-05-13-c) didn't test the full flow end-to-end before declaring the design "verified" (the session_2026-05-13-c CHAT_REGISTRY entry explicitly said "live end-to-end run deferred to next session — first real run IS the next session's launch, which doubles as the verification"). When the first real run finally happened today, the bug surfaced.

**Why this is worth recording.** Two distinct lessons:

(1) **Don't ship a CLI-integration design without testing the full flow on a real session start.** The 2026-05-13-c session deferred verification with the rationale "first real run IS the verification" — but if the first real run fails (as today did), the failure surfaces in front of the director instead of in a controlled test. Better discipline: when a new mechanism touches CLI invocation, sandbox-test it (e.g., `bash -x ./resume` with a faked NEXT_SESSION.md + observe whether claude actually receives the prompt) BEFORE declaring shipped. Today's fix verifies via Claude Code's `claude --help` output explicitly noting the `[prompt]` positional is for "starts interactive session by default, use -p/--print for non-interactive output" — i.e., the documented behavior didn't promise auto-submit in interactive mode in the first place.

(2) **Single-mechanism designs are structurally fragile for user-facing redundancy promises.** Director's framing: *"despite many redundancies in place, it looks like the methodology you applied still led to issues."* The original `./resume` design had only one path: positional `[prompt]` auto-submit. When that path was broken, there was no fallback layer; the "redundancy" was nominal not real. The fix replaces this with four composed layers (Rule 28 multi-layered defense): SessionStart hook (primary, mechanical) + sentinel-string match (procedural fallback) + ESCAPE HATCH 3-step path (manual fallback) + NEXT_SESSION.md guard hook (end-of-session enforcement). Each layer covers a different failure mode of the others; at least one layer is always available.

**Lesson.** **When shipping any director-facing automation that promises N redundancies, count the actual independent layers that produce the same outcome — not the number of files/docs/rules that reference the mechanism.** "Redundancy" in design language means "if X fails, Y still produces the right outcome." Five docs all describing the same single-path mechanism is one layer, not five. Today's fix is genuinely multi-layered because each layer has a different failure mode + a different recovery path.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — the bug forced director to do extra work (manual paste) on every `./resume` use until fixed; not destructive but a real UX failure that violated the stated design promise. Fixed same session via Rule 28 + the file changes catalogued in `CHAT_REGISTRY.md` 2026-05-14 follow-up entry.

**Corrected in this session's follow-up batch:**
- NEW `.claude/hooks/inject-next-session-pointer.sh` (the SessionStart hook — Layer 1)
- UPDATED `.claude/settings.json` (wired the new SessionStart hook alongside the existing PreToolUse guard hook)
- UPDATED `./resume` (removed broken `"$SENTINEL"` positional arg from `exec claude` line; improved terminal-side guidance for the wake-up keystroke)
- UPDATED `docs/CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` "Resume-flow handling" section (now describes the multi-layered defense)
- NEW `docs/HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 28 (codifies the multi-layered defense + test discipline)
- UPDATED `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` (adds the end-to-end test plan for the next session — verify the fix works before doing P-20 design work)
- Fix landed on both `main` AND `workflow-2-competition-scraping` (merged from main) so the hook + settings + script are present regardless of which branch `./resume` checks out.

**Cross-references.** `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 28 (the canonical Rule for the multi-layered defense); `CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` "Resume-flow handling" section (the user-facing description); session_2026-05-13-c CHAT_REGISTRY entry (the original ship that had the design flaw + the explicit deferral of end-to-end verification that allowed the flaw to ship undetected); `claude --help` output (the canonical documentation that the positional `[prompt]` only auto-submits in print mode).

### 2026-05-14 — Launch-prompt staleness pattern: pasted launch prompt described state main had moved past (INFORMATIONAL — start-of-session drift caught cleanly)

**What happened.** Director's pasted launch prompt at session start said *"W#2 → main deploy session #10 — bring session 5 + session 6 Module 2 image-capture (regular-image + region-screenshot, both gestures) to vklf.com … both unverified on vklf.com."* But main had already moved past that state: session 5's regular-image gesture had been deployed in commit `bd7b39a` (deploy-#10) and cross-platform fully verified in commit `907a363` (a.24-verify-COMPLETE) — both already on main when today's session started. The launch prompt's premise of "both unverified" no longer held; only session 6's region-screenshot was unverified.

**Why it happened.** The launch prompt was generated by `docs/NEXT_SESSION.md` on the `workflow-2-competition-scraping` branch in the prior session-6 follow-up commit (`51f57cc`). Between then and today's session start, main got 3 new commits in parallel: `3ae0a80` (./resume + NEXT_SESSION.md platform-wide ship), `479b192` (NEXT_SESSION.md finalize after §4 Step 1c interview), and `907a363` ((a.24) cross-platform verification COMPLETE doc batch). The director's pointer file was a snapshot in time; the receiving branch had since updated independently.

**How it surfaced.** During the mandatory start-of-session sequence, `git log origin/main -5` showed 3 new commits since the workflow-2 branch's last sync point, including the verification-COMPLETE entry. The drift surfaced before any code work began; Claude flagged it in the drift check and surfaced a Rule 14f forced-picker with 3 options (reconcile + deploy session 6 only [recommended] / cherry-pick session 6 only / pause + revise scope). Director picked recommended Option A.

**Lesson.** **At start of every W#2 → main deploy session (or any rebase-then-ff session), the FIRST git command after branch verification should be `git log origin/main..workflow-2-X` AND `git log workflow-2-X..origin/main` to surface parallel-branch divergence BEFORE reading the launch prompt's framing as authoritative.** The launch prompt is a snapshot of intent at the time the prior session ended; the actual branch state is the canonical truth. When the two disagree, drift-check + surface to director via forced-picker is the right move (don't silently proceed assuming the launch prompt is correct).

**Why this is worth recording.** Parallel-branch sessions accumulate state in BOTH branches between the prior session's "next session" pointer-write and the next session's actual start. The longer the gap (or the more sessions that happen on either branch in the meantime), the more drift accumulates. Without an explicit "compare both directions of `origin/main..workflow-2-X`" check at session start, the launch prompt's stale framing can drive Claude into doing the wrong work (in this case, re-verifying session 5 unnecessarily). Caught cleanly today; future sessions should make this check a first-class action of the start-of-session sequence rather than relying on Claude noticing it ad-hoc.

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — no production impact; drift caught before any work; recovery was a forced-picker that produced the correct scope on the first try.

**Cross-references.** `CLAUDE_CODE_STARTER.md` Step 2 branch verification (existing rule that runs `git branch --show-current`); `MULTI_WORKFLOW_PROTOCOL.md` §6 (existing dual-direction pull-rebase pattern); `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 25 (Multi-workflow coordination). Today's session's pre-work commit-divergence check (`git log origin/main..HEAD` + `git log HEAD..origin/main`) is the pattern future deploy sessions should follow at start-of-session.

---

### 2026-05-14 — Doc-batch-empty-on-rebase pattern: parallel-branch doc-batch commits become empty when rebased onto receiving branch's parallel doc batch (INFORMATIONAL — clean operational pattern)

**What happened.** During the rebase of 7 workflow-2-competition-scraping commits onto current origin/main HEAD `907a363`, two commits dropped during replay: (1) commit `8c39f20` "Merge branch 'main' into workflow-2-competition-scraping" was naturally collapsed by the non-merge rebase (expected — merge commits don't replay in `git rebase`); (2) commit `68fd93d` ((a.24-a) P-22 slice 2 end-of-session doc batch) became EMPTY after I took `git checkout --ours` for all its 5 conflict files (CHAT_REGISTRY + COMPETITION_SCRAPING_VERIFICATION_BACKLOG + DOCUMENT_MANIFEST + ROADMAP). `git rebase --continue` silently dropped the empty commit. Net effect: 7 workflow-2 commits became 5 commits on the rebased branch (one merge collapsed + one doc-batch dropped as empty).

**Why it happened.** The (a.24-a) doc-batch commit was written on the workflow-2 branch in parallel with main's (a.24-verify-COMPLETE) doc-batch commit. Both sessions prepended doc entries to the same files (CHAT_REGISTRY "Last updated", ROADMAP "Last updated", DOCUMENT_MANIFEST "Last updated", VERIFICATION_BACKLOG "Last updated"). When main's `907a363` already had its prepended (a.24) entry, the workflow-2's (a.24-a) prepend conflicted with it at the same exact slot. Taking `--ours` (HEAD's already-updated docs) discarded workflow-2's prepend; since the rest of the (a.24-a) commit was that same prepend in 5 files, the commit had zero net diff against HEAD and became empty.

**Why this is worth recording.** Future W#2 → main deploy sessions facing similar parallel-branch divergence should EXPECT 0–2 doc-batch commits to drop as empty during rebase. This is the EXPECTED CLEAN OPERATIONAL PATTERN, not a bug or a slip. The lost content isn't actually lost — it's already in HEAD (via the parallel session's prepend); just the duplicate "previous-update" entry that would have appeared if both sessions had landed sequentially is what's dropped.

**Lesson — operational policy for parallel-branch doc-batch commits during rebase:**
- When a doc-batch commit's conflicts are 100% prepends to "Last updated" slots that HEAD has already prepended itself, take `git checkout --ours` for the conflicted files.
- Run `git diff --cached --stat HEAD` after staging to confirm the diff is empty.
- Run `git rebase --continue` — git will silently drop the empty commit (no error).
- In the receiving session's end-of-session doc batch, document the count: "N commits brought via rebase; M dropped (K empty after --ours per the doc-batch-empty pattern; J merge commits naturally collapsed by non-merge rebase)."

**Caveat.** Don't reflexively `--ours` doc-batch conflicts if the workflow-branch's commit had OTHER content too (code changes, new sections, schema, etc.). The `--ours` shortcut is only safe when the entire commit's diff is pure "prepend to N doc-log files" — verify via `git show --stat <commit-being-replayed>` showing only docs/* with small +/- counts. If the commit had code changes too, manual conflict resolution per file is required (preserve the workflow's code intent while reconciling the doc-prepend with HEAD's parallel entry).

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — clean operational pattern; no production impact; no doc content lost (HEAD already has equivalent prepends from the parallel session).

**Cross-references.** Today's rebase: `3b58e29` P-22 slice 1 (replayed clean) + `2ad1ff2` (a.23) doc batch (replayed with manual conflict resolution since (a.23)'s content was distinct from any HEAD entry) + `8c39f20` merge (collapsed) + `d1ac234` P-22 slice 2 (replayed clean) + `68fd93d` (a.24-a) doc batch (became empty + dropped) + `516c124` session 6 (replayed with manual conflict resolution for HANDOFF_PROTOCOL row-12 augment + --ours for other docs) + `51f57cc` Follow-up (replayed with manual conflict resolution for P-23/P-24/P-25 polish entries + --ours for shared `./resume` + `NEXT_SESSION.md`). Net: 7 → 5 commits. Future deploy sessions: this is the expected pattern when the receiving branch has accumulated parallel doc-batch entries.

---

### 2026-05-14 — Rule 27 forced-picker not run before proposing manual cross-platform smoke walkthrough (INFORMATIONAL — verification-session start slip)

**What happened.** At start of session, after the doc-drift re-scope was completed, I went directly to walking the director through the manual eBay walkthrough WITHOUT running the Rule 27 forced-picker comparing Playwright extension-context vs. manual walkthrough vs. hybrid. Surfaced mid-session when director asked: *"Did you already check these things yourself using Playwright or some other tool?"* I retroactively ran the Rule 27 forced-picker, which surfaced **Option B Hybrid** (Claude runs server-side DB checks via a new inspection script + director does in-browser UI clicks) — director picked B. The Hybrid path proved valuable: DB inspection confirmed yesterday's Walmart row state (surfaced the separate doc-drift slip below) and after each platform's UI smoke the new row's correct metadata (platform/URL/imageCategory/mimeType/storagePath) was verified server-side — catching a class of bugs that pure UI verification can miss.

**Why it happened.** The pointer file had recorded "Director picked Option C Hybrid at the original P-22 capture moment — manual smoke first, Playwright spec deferred to its own session; honor that pick unless director redirects." I interpreted this as "the forced-picker was already done; just proceed with manual" — but Rule 27 says the picker fires on EVERY manual-walkthrough proposal moment, not just the first-time-ever one. A prior session's pick doesn't preempt the current session's picker, especially when the current session has DIFFERENT options available (server-side DB inspection wasn't on the original P-22 picker's option set).

**Why this is worth recording.** Rule 27 specifically targets the "let me just walk you through it manually" reflex. The slip pattern: prior session has a Hybrid pick → current session reads pointer file's "honor that pick" framing → skips re-running the picker → loses the chance to surface automation paths that didn't exist at the original picker moment.

**Lesson.** **At every manual-walkthrough proposal moment, re-run the Rule 27 forced-picker even if the pointer file references a prior pick.** The option set today may include automation paths that weren't available at the original picker moment. Honoring the prior pick should be the DEFAULT after running the picker, not a substitute for running it.

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — no production impact; verification still got done thoroughly via the retroactive picker run.

**Cross-references.** `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 27 (Playwright forced-picker); `feedback_playwright_for_repeatable_walkthroughs.md` (operational memory).

---

### 2026-05-14 — Ran prereq state checks at session start before clarifying actual scope-from-yesterday (INFORMATIONAL — verification-session start slip)

**What happened.** At session start, after producing the drift check and getting "Go ahead," I started Task #1 (Walmart form rendering) by walking the director through prereq state checks: Step 1 verify extension still loaded; Step 2 verify signed in to vklf.com; Step 3 open Walmart product page. Director pushed back: *"What are you making me test here? We had already tested adding images by right clicking on walmart platform and it worked."* The pushback surfaced major doc drift (separate entry below): yesterday's session 2026-05-13-b had completed FULL Walmart end-to-end verification (3 CapturedImage rows confirmed in DB via the new inspection script), but the docs/pointer file had recorded it as "PARTIAL verify, context-menu firing only." The prereq checks I started with were appropriate for a "we're starting fresh on Walmart" framing — but the actual state was "Walmart is done; only cross-platform smoke remains."

**Why it happened.** I trusted the pointer file's "yesterday only verified context-menu firing" framing as authoritative without surfacing it as a clarification question to the director at session start. Per Rule 14g (trust director's setup confirmation) + Rule 1 (verify before write, with smaller confirmation checkpoints) + Rule 14a (read-it-back test), I should have surfaced something like "the pointer file says yesterday only verified context-menu firing — is that accurate, or did you go further?" BEFORE proposing prereq steps. The clarification question was the load-bearing first move; the prereq steps were premature.

**Why this is worth recording.** Verification sessions that reference prior-session state are a common pattern. The doc-vs-actual-state gap is a structural risk — yesterday's session can wrap mid-walkthrough due to environment issues (terminal-UI rendering, fatigue, time pressure) and the doc batch may not fully reflect what was actually accomplished before the wrap. The right move is to TREAT the pointer file's scope assertion as a clarification anchor, not a fact, and confirm with director before proposing the first verification step.

**Lesson.** **At verification-session start, when the pointer file references prior-session state, surface a scope-clarification question to the director BEFORE running prereq checks or proposing the first verification step.** Frame: "the pointer file says yesterday accomplished X — is that accurate?" Director's affirmation/correction is authoritative per Rule 14g; docs are not.

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — no production impact; doc drift was caught and corrected this session.

**Cross-references.** `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 1 (verify before write) + Rule 14a (read-it-back) + Rule 14g (trust director's setup confirmation); `feedback_trust_director_setup_confirmation.md` (operational memory).

---

### 2026-05-14 — Doc drift carried from 2026-05-13-b end-of-session batch — deploy-#10 PARTIAL → actually FULL Walmart verify (INFORMATIONAL — wrap-condition slip carried from prior session)

**What happened.** Yesterday's session 2026-05-13-b end-of-session doc batch recorded deploy-#10 (Module 2 regular-image gesture) as "DEPLOYED + PARTIAL VERIFY — context-menu firing only verified on Walmart; form rendering + 2-phase upload + CapturedImage row + cross-platform smoke DEFERRED to next session." Today's DB inspection via the new `scripts/inspect-w2-state.mjs` revealed 3 CapturedImage rows for the Walmart Equate URL (`/ip/17056909`) from 2026-05-13-b across 3 different metadata patterns (full-form Main Image with Composition + Embedded text + Tags; minimum-form Secondary image; tags-only Secondary image). Director confirmed: yesterday's actual work went well beyond context-menu firing; it completed the full Walmart flow with multiple metadata variations. The doc batch missed this.

**Why it happened (per session 2026-05-13-b's own self-report).** Yesterday's session wrapped at director's request mid-walkthrough due to a "terminal-UI rendering issue making my step instructions hard to read." The session ended before Claude could write up the form/save/upload verifications that had already been completed BEFORE the rendering issue surfaced. The end-of-session doc batch captured only the LAST recorded checkpoint (context-menu firing) as the verification state, missing the post-context-menu work that director had actually completed.

**Why this is worth recording.** Sessions that wrap due to environment issues (terminal rendering, fatigue, time pressure) can leave doc drift between "what session reported as verified" and "what director actually verified." The verification-state record in the doc batch is the source of truth for future sessions; if it understates actual verification, future sessions will redo work that was already done or operate on incorrect state.

**Lesson.** **When a session wraps mid-walkthrough due to environment issues, the doc batch must explicitly account for verifications completed BEFORE the wrap, not just the last logged checkpoint.** This requires Claude to trace back what was verified earlier in the session (re-read the user's confirmations) and reflect it accurately in the end-of-session VERIFICATION_BACKLOG section + pointer file + ROADMAP row, not assume "wrap point = end of verification."

**Severity.** INFORMATIONAL — no production impact; today's session caught and corrected the drift via DB inspection.

**Corrected in this session's doc batch:** VERIFICATION_BACKLOG header + new "FULL VERIFY + cross-platform smoke COMPLETE 2026-05-14" section preserves the original 2026-05-13-b "PARTIAL VERIFY" section as historical record + ROADMAP W#2 row (a.23) flipped to ✅ DONE FULL VERIFY + pointer file rewritten for (a.24).

**Cross-references.** `HANDOFF_PROTOCOL.md` Rule 3 (code is source of truth — DB inspection > stale doc) + Rule 14e (capture what you defer — including verifications completed-not-documented); `feedback_session_management.md` (operational memory — wrap before degrading discipline).
